questions.explanation: The Sun is the ultimate energy source for nearly all ecosystems on Earth, driving photosynthesis and powering the food chain from producers upward. Solar radiation also drives the water cycle, weather systems, and atmospheric circulation (Hadley cells) — making it the master variable for climate and biogeography. Only a few chemosynthetic ecosystems (deep-sea hydrothermal vents, discovered 1977 by Alvin submersible at the Galapagos Rift) bypass the Sun. On AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 1.2), students must trace solar energy from incoming insolation through NPP into trophic-level biomass and explain why only ~1% of incoming solar energy is captured by photosynthesis.

questions.prompt: Process producers use to capture light.
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6 CO2 + 6 H2O + light energy yields C6H12O6 + 6 O2. Discovered by Joseph Priestley (1771, oxygen release) and Jan Ingenhousz (1779, light requirement). Captures roughly 1-3% of incoming solar energy as chemical bonds in glucose. Drives gross primary productivity (GPP); net primary productivity (NPP) = GPP minus plant respiration. On the AP Environmental Science FRQ, students must memorize the equation (CED Unit 1.3) and explain that tropical rainforests have the highest NPP (~2,200 g C/m^2/yr) while deserts and tundra have the lowest (<200). Source of all biospheric O2.

Producers (autotrophs) sit at trophic level 1 and make their own organic compounds, primarily via photosynthesis. They form the energetic base of every food web — without producers there is no higher trophic structure. Plants on land, phytoplankton in the oceans (responsible for ~50% of global photosynthesis per NASA SeaWiFS data), algae in freshwater, and chemosynthetic bacteria around hydrothermal vents are all producers. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 1.4) test biomass pyramid construction starting with producers and apply the 10% rule upward — a 10,000 kg producer base supports only 1 kg of tertiary consumers.

Primary consumers (herbivores) occupy trophic level 2, eating producers directly. Examples: deer browsing forest understory, zooplankton grazing phytoplankton, caterpillars eating leaves, cattle on rangeland. They receive only ~10% of producer energy (Lindeman's 10% rule, 1942). On AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 1.6), students construct energy-flow diagrams showing 90% energy loss as heat at each trophic transfer. Primary consumers are critical for ecosystem stability — overgrazing (e.g., elk in Yellowstone before wolf reintroduction 1995) reshapes plant communities through top-down trophic cascades.

questions.prompt: Rule for energy efficiency between levels.
questions.explanation: The 10% rule (Raymond Lindeman, 1942) states that only ~10% of the energy at one trophic level is incorporated as biomass at the next level; ~90% is lost as metabolic heat via cellular respiration, undigested waste, and incomplete consumption. This is why food chains rarely exceed 4-5 levels — energy runs out. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 1.7) demand quantitative application: if producers fix 100,000 kcal/m^2/yr, herbivores get 10,000, primary carnivores 1,000, secondary carnivores 100. Explains why eating lower on the food chain is energetically more efficient — a key argument for plant-based diets.

A food chain is a linear sequence of organisms in which each is eaten by the next: producer to primary consumer to secondary consumer to tertiary consumer to decomposer. Example: grass to grasshopper to frog to snake to hawk. Charles Elton (1927, 'Animal Ecology') formalized the concept and introduced the term 'food chain.' Each link is a trophic transfer with ~90% energy loss. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 1.6) ask students to construct a food chain from a habitat description and apply the 10% rule to calculate biomass at each level. Food chains rarely have more than 4-5 links due to energy attrition.

A food web is the interconnected network of multiple food chains in an ecosystem — most organisms eat and are eaten by several species, creating a web rather than a single chain. Elton (1927) recognized that food webs better represent real ecological complexity than simple chains. Removing a single species (e.g., sea otters off California, hunted to near-extinction by 1900) can cascade through the web — without otters, sea urchins exploded and decimated kelp forests, demonstrating keystone-species effects. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 1.6) require students to predict cascade effects from species removal and identify keystone species.

A trophic level is an organism's position in the food chain based on its energy source: Level 1 = producers, Level 2 = primary consumers (herbivores), Level 3 = secondary consumers (carnivores eating herbivores), Level 4 = tertiary consumers (top predators), and decomposers cycling all levels. Each level loses ~90% of energy as heat (10% rule, Lindeman 1942). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 1.7) require students to compute biomass at each trophic level using the 10% rule and explain why pyramids of biomass and energy narrow upward. Omnivores (e.g., humans, bears) occupy multiple trophic levels simultaneously.

Tertiary consumers are apex predators at the top of the food chain — examples include bald eagles, sharks, polar bears, lions, orcas, and humans. They have no natural predators and regulate populations below them via top-down control. Because they sit highest, they accumulate the most pollutant biomagnification: DDT concentrations in bald eagles reached 25 ppm in the 1960s versus 0.0001 ppm in water (Rachel Carson, 'Silent Spring' 1962), causing eggshell thinning and population collapse. The 1972 DDT ban under the EPA recovered eagle populations. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 8.7) chain tertiary consumers with biomagnification calculations.

Decomposers — bacteria, fungi, and detritivores (earthworms, beetles, vultures) — break down dead organic matter, returning nutrients (N, P, C) to soil and atmosphere. Without them, nutrient cycling halts and ecosystems collapse. They form the foundation of the soil food web and complete every biogeochemical cycle. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 1.9, 1.10) require students to trace carbon and nitrogen through decomposers back to producers. In the carbon cycle, decomposers release ~50 Gt C/yr globally via respiration of dead biomass — comparable to fossil fuel emissions. Decomposition rates vary by climate: fast in tropics (high T, moisture), slow in tundra (cold preserves organic peat).

The carbon cycle moves C between atmosphere (CO2), biosphere (organic compounds), hydrosphere (dissolved CO2, HCO3-), and lithosphere (limestone, fossil fuels). Major fluxes: photosynthesis removes ~120 Gt C/yr from atmosphere; respiration + decomposition returns ~120 Gt C/yr; fossil fuel combustion adds ~10 Gt C/yr (anthropogenic perturbation, Keeling Curve at Mauna Loa since 1958 documents +50% CO2 rise to ~420 ppm in 2024). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 1.9) require students to identify sources and sinks and explain how deforestation + fossil fuels shift the balance toward atmospheric accumulation, driving climate change.

The nitrogen cycle: atmospheric N2 (78% of air) is unusable by most organisms until 'fixed' into NH3/NH4+ by Rhizobium bacteria in legume root nodules (or by lightning, or industrially via Haber-Bosch process, Fritz Haber Nobel 1918). Plants take up NH4+ and NO3-, animals eat plants, decomposers ammonify dead organic matter, nitrifying bacteria convert NH4+ to NO3-, and denitrifying bacteria return N2 to atmosphere. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 1.10) require students to name all five processes (fixation, nitrification, assimilation, ammonification, denitrification) and explain how synthetic fertilizers cause eutrophication and dead zones (Gulf of Mexico).

The phosphorus cycle has NO atmospheric component — P moves slowly through rock weathering (release of PO4^3- from apatite), uptake by plants, transfer through food chains, decomposition, and sedimentation back to rock over millions of years. This makes P the limiting nutrient in many freshwater ecosystems. Mined phosphate rock (mostly from Morocco/Western Sahara, ~75% of global reserves) provides synthetic fertilizer; runoff causes lake eutrophication (e.g., Lake Erie algal blooms 2014 forcing Toledo OH water shutdown). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 1.11) test the difference from the N cycle (no atmospheric phase) and predict eutrophication from agricultural P loading.

The hydrologic (water) cycle moves H2O between atmosphere, surface water, groundwater, and ice via evaporation, transpiration, condensation, precipitation, infiltration, and runoff — powered by solar energy and gravity. Global flux: ~500,000 km^3/yr evaporates from oceans; ~110,000 km^3/yr falls on land as precipitation. Only 2.5% of Earth's water is freshwater, and just 0.3% is accessible surface freshwater. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 4.9) require students to label diagrams with all 6 processes and explain how deforestation reduces transpiration, climate change intensifies the cycle (warmer air holds more moisture per Clausius-Clapeyron, +7%/degC), and aquifer depletion (Ogallala) disrupts the groundwater leg.

Nitrogen fixation: N2 + 3 H2 yields 2 NH3 — converts inert atmospheric N2 (which has a strong triple bond, 945 kJ/mol) into bioavailable ammonia. Biologically performed by Rhizobium in legume root nodules, cyanobacteria, and Azotobacter; abiotically by lightning (~5%) and industrially by the Haber-Bosch process (Fritz Haber and Carl Bosch, Nobel 1918/1931) which now fixes more N annually than all natural processes combined (~120 Tg/yr industrial vs ~110 Tg/yr biological). Without Haber-Bosch, half of humanity would starve (Vaclav Smil). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 1.10) require students to identify Rhizobium as the key bacterial genus and explain crop-rotation logic — alternating legumes with corn restores soil N.

Ecosystem services are the free, often invisible benefits humans receive from functioning ecosystems. The 2005 Millennium Ecosystem Assessment categorized them into four classes: provisioning (food, water, fiber), regulating (climate, flood, disease control), supporting (nutrient cycling, soil formation, photosynthesis), and cultural (recreation, aesthetic, spiritual). Economist Robert Costanza (1997 Nature paper) estimated global ecosystem-service value at $33 trillion/year — roughly double global GDP at the time. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 1.5) require students to categorize specific services (e.g., bee pollination = provisioning + regulating; mangrove storm protection = regulating) and argue economic justifications for conservation.

Provisioning services are tangible goods extracted from ecosystems: food (crops, fish, game), fresh water, fuelwood, fiber (cotton, timber), genetic resources, and medicinal plants. Bee pollination (by Apis mellifera and native pollinators) supports ~75% of leading global food crops and is valued at $235-577 billion/year globally (IPBES 2016). Bee colony collapse disorder (CCD), first documented 2006, threatens provisioning. Wild fisheries provide ~17% of global animal protein but ~33% of stocks are overfished (FAO 2020). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 1.5) require students to recognize concrete provisioning examples and contrast them with regulating services like climate stabilization.

Regulating services maintain environmental conditions essential for life: climate regulation (forests absorb CO2 at ~7 Gt/yr), flood control (wetlands store water, reducing peak flow ~60%), water purification (forested watersheds remove pollutants), disease regulation (intact ecosystems suppress pathogen reservoirs), and pollination. Coastal wetlands and mangroves dampen storm surge — post-Hurricane Sandy (2012) analyses estimated $625 million in damages averted by surviving NE wetlands. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 1.5) require students to distinguish regulating from provisioning (regulating = process; provisioning = goods) and predict consequences when regulating services fail (e.g., Mississippi flooding 1993 after wetland drainage).

Cultural services are non-material benefits: aesthetic enjoyment, recreation (hiking, fishing, ecotourism), spiritual and religious values, educational opportunities, and sense of place. Yellowstone National Park (established 1872 as the world's first national park by President Grant) generates $647 million/year in visitor spending — a measurable economic value of cultural services. Ecotourism in Costa Rica accounts for ~10% of GDP. Indigenous communities derive irreplaceable spiritual value from specific landscapes (e.g., Bears Ears, Mauna Kea). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 1.5) require students to argue that cultural services, while difficult to monetize, justify protected-area expansion and biodiversity conservation.

Supporting services underlie all other ecosystem services: soil formation (1 cm of topsoil takes ~500-1000 years to build), nutrient cycling (N, P, C), primary production via photosynthesis, oxygen production, and habitat provision. They differ from other services because they operate slowly and indirectly — their benefits are realized through other services rather than directly. Loss of supporting services has compounding effects: soil erosion (Dust Bowl 1930s, 75 million acres degraded) eliminates the substrate for provisioning. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 1.5) require students to recognize supporting services as foundational and explain why their loss is harder to reverse than loss of other service classes.

A biome is a large geographic region characterized by its dominant climate, vegetation, and animal communities. The eight major terrestrial biomes (tundra, taiga, temperate deciduous forest, temperate grassland, tropical rainforest, savanna, desert, chaparral) are determined primarily by temperature and precipitation, captured in Whittaker's 1975 climograph. Latitude, altitude, and proximity to oceans create the global pattern. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 1.1) require students to identify biomes from climate data, predict shifts under climate change (e.g., boreal forest expanding into former tundra as permafrost thaws), and connect biome to NPP (rainforest highest at ~2,200 g C/m^2/yr, tundra lowest at ~140).

Deserts receive <25 cm of precipitation per year and span ~30% of Earth's land surface. They occur at ~30 degree N/S latitudes where descending dry Hadley cell air creates arid conditions (Sahara, Sonoran, Atacama). Plants and animals show extreme adaptations: cacti store water and reduce surface area to lower transpiration (CAM photosynthesis), kangaroo rats produce highly concentrated urine. The Sahel region of Africa is experiencing desertification (UN definition: persistent land degradation in drylands) at ~1% per year due to overgrazing, deforestation, and climate change. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 1.1) require students to identify desert biome characteristics and connect to climate-driven biome shifts.

Tundra is a treeless biome found at high latitudes (Arctic) or high altitudes (alpine), characterized by permafrost (permanently frozen subsoil), <25 cm precipitation, short growing seasons (~50-60 days), and low NPP (~140 g C/m^2/yr — among Earth's lowest). Dominant plants: mosses, lichens, sedges, low shrubs. Permafrost stores ~1,500 Gt C (twice atmospheric content) — climate warming is thawing it, releasing CH4 and CO2 in a positive feedback that accelerates global warming. Methane is ~25x more potent as a greenhouse gas than CO2 (100-year GWP). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 1.1, 9.4) integrate tundra characteristics with carbon-cycle feedback loops.

Tropical rainforests are Earth's most biodiverse biome: while occupying only ~6% of land surface, they harbor 50-80% of all terrestrial species (E.O. Wilson, 'The Diversity of Life,' 1992). Located near the equator (Amazon, Congo, SE Asia), they receive >200 cm annual rainfall, average 25-27 degC year-round, and produce the highest NPP (~2,200 g C/m^2/yr). Wilson estimated species loss from tropical deforestation at 27,000/yr in the 1990s — the modern 'sixth extinction.' Deforestation rate has slowed but still claims ~10 million ha/yr globally. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 1.1, 2.5) integrate biome NPP with biodiversity hotspots and Wilson's quantitative species-area predictions.

Grasslands (temperate prairie + tropical savanna) receive 25-75 cm of annual rainfall — too little for forest, too much for desert. The North American tallgrass prairie once covered 170 million acres; today <4% remains (most converted to corn/wheat agriculture by the Homestead Act 1862 and post-1870 plowing). The Dust Bowl (1930s) followed deep plowing of prairie sod — 75 million acres degraded, prompting the Soil Conservation Act of 1935 and creation of the Soil Conservation Service under Hugh Hammond Bennett. Tropical savannas (African Serengeti) support the largest herbivore migrations on Earth. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 1.1, 5.2) connect grassland conversion to topsoil loss, NPP changes, and biodiversity decline.

questions.prompt: Variety of life in an area.
questions.explanation: Biodiversity is the variety of life at three nested scales: genetic diversity (alleles within species), species diversity (number and abundance of species in a community), and ecosystem diversity (variety of habitats). E.O. Wilson, the 'father of biodiversity' (he coined and popularized the term in his 1988 edited volume 'BioDiversity'), estimated 8.7 million species exist (only ~2 million described). Wilson argued biodiversity has intrinsic, instrumental, and aesthetic value. The Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD, 1992 Rio Earth Summit) is the global treaty. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 2.1) require students to define all three scales and connect biodiversity to ecosystem resilience and ecosystem services.

questions.prompt: Number of species in an area.
questions.explanation: Species richness is the simple count of different species in a defined area, the most basic biodiversity metric. Tropical rainforests (Amazon, Congo) show highest richness — a single hectare can contain 300+ tree species (E.O. Wilson). Latitudinal diversity gradient: richness peaks near equator, declines toward poles, established by Alexander von Humboldt (1807). Species-area relationship: S = c A^z (Arrhenius 1921; Wilson and MacArthur applied to island biogeography 1967) — doubling area increases richness by ~2^0.25 = 19%. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 2.2) require students to compute richness from data tables and contrast it with Shannon-Wiener and Simpson indices that incorporate evenness.

Species evenness measures how equitably individuals are distributed among species. A community with 100 individuals split as 50-50 between two species is more even than 99-1. Combined with richness in the Shannon-Wiener index H = -sum(p_i * ln p_i), which AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 2.2) frequently require students to calculate. A monoculture cornfield has high crop density but evenness near zero (essentially one species dominates); old-growth forest has moderate richness with high evenness. Low evenness signals disturbance, invasion, or eutrophication. Margalef (1958) showed that successional communities increase in evenness as they mature toward climax.

Genetic diversity is the variation in alleles within a species — the raw material of evolution and adaptation. Low genetic diversity from inbreeding, founder effects, or population bottlenecks reduces fitness and increases extinction risk. Classic case: the Florida panther dropped to ~30 individuals in the 1990s with severe inbreeding (cardiac defects, low sperm count); Texas cougar introductions in 1995 restored genetic diversity and the population rebounded. Cheetahs have famously low genetic diversity (Pleistocene bottleneck). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 2.4) require students to explain why monoculture crops (Irish potato 1845-49 famine, Cavendish bananas threatened by Panama disease) are catastrophically vulnerable to pathogens.

Habitat (ecosystem) diversity is the variety of distinct ecosystems within a larger region — wetlands, forests, grasslands, streams, etc. — each supporting different communities. Higher habitat diversity supports higher overall species richness because each habitat hosts unique specialists. The Everglades National Park (established 1947) contains sawgrass prairies, cypress domes, mangrove estuaries, and pine rocklands — protecting habitat diversity protects regional biodiversity. Habitat fragmentation reduces this diversity. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 2.1, 2.6) require students to argue that conservation must protect multiple habitat types, not just one large reserve — connecting to MacArthur and Wilson's 1967 island biogeography theory (larger and closer 'islands' hold more species).

Mutualism is a (+/+) interaction where both species benefit. Classic examples: mycorrhizal fungi colonize ~90% of plant roots, exchanging mineral nutrients (P, N) for plant sugars — a partnership dating to ~400 million years ago that enabled plant colonization of land. Honeybees and flowering plants (nectar for pollination). Acacia trees and stinging ants (food and shelter for ant defense, Daniel Janzen 1966 field experiments). Coral reefs depend on mutualism between coral polyps and Symbiodinium dinoflagellate algae — when stressed (warm water), corals expel algae causing bleaching. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 2.3) require students to predict cascade effects when a mutualist is removed.

Commensalism is a (+/0) interaction where one species benefits and the other is unaffected. Examples: barnacles attached to whales (transport benefit; whales unharmed), cattle egrets following grazing herbivores (catching disturbed insects without affecting cattle), epiphytic orchids growing on tropical-rainforest tree branches (height/light access without harming host). Many 'commensal' relationships actually involve subtle mutualism or parasitism on closer inspection — true (+/0) interactions are rare. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 2.3) require students to categorize species interactions into the five canonical types: mutualism (+/+), commensalism (+/0), competition (-/-), predation (+/-), parasitism (+/-).

Parasitism is a (+/-) interaction where one organism benefits at the host's expense, typically without immediate killing (distinguishes from predation). Tapeworms, mosquitoes, fleas, plant rusts. Parasites often coevolve with hosts toward reduced virulence — killing your host ends the relationship. Notable invasive parasites: emerald ash borer (since 2002 in N. America) has killed >100 million ash trees; chestnut blight (Cryphonectria parasitica, accidentally introduced 1904) functionally extirpated the American chestnut from eastern forests. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 2.3, 9.6) require students to identify parasitism's role in biodiversity dynamics and predict cascade effects when key host species decline.

Predation is a (+/-) interaction where one species (predator) kills and consumes another (prey). Drives population cycles famously documented by the Lotka-Volterra equations (Alfred Lotka 1925, Vito Volterra 1926) and observed in lynx-hare populations from Hudson's Bay Company fur records (~10-year cycle). Top-down predator control creates trophic cascades: gray wolves reintroduced to Yellowstone National Park (1995, after 70-year absence) reduced elk grazing pressure on aspen and willow, restored riparian vegetation, and changed even river morphology — a textbook AP Environmental Science FRQ example of trophic cascades (CED Unit 2.3). Predation maintains community structure and biodiversity.

questions.prompt: Two species compete for same resource.
questions.explanation: Competition is a (-/-) interaction where two species or individuals contest a limiting resource (food, water, space, light). Gause's competitive exclusion principle (Russian ecologist Georgy Gause, 1934 Paramecium experiments): two species cannot coexist indefinitely on identical limiting resources — one wins, one is excluded or shifts niche (resource partitioning, MacArthur's 1958 warbler study). Invasive species often outcompete natives because they lack co-evolved predators/diseases: zebra mussels (Dreissena polymorpha, arrived 1988 via Great Lakes ballast water) displaced native mussels and cost $5+ billion in infrastructure damage. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 2.3, 9.6) require students to predict competitive outcomes from niche overlap.

Ecological succession is the directional, predictable change in community composition over time following disturbance. Frederic Clements (1916) proposed the deterministic 'climax' model — communities march toward a single stable end-state determined by climate. Henry Gleason (1926) countered with the individualistic model — communities are accidents of species dispersal and tolerance. Modern view synthesizes both. Succession increases biomass, biodiversity, and complexity over time. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 2.7) require students to distinguish primary from secondary succession, name pioneer species (lichens, mosses), trace successional stages (annuals to perennials to shrubs to early-successional trees to climax forest), and predict timelines (decades to centuries).

Primary succession begins on lifeless substrate with no pre-existing soil — bare rock from glacial retreat, volcanic lava, or new sand dunes. Pioneer species (lichens, then mosses) slowly weather rock and build initial soil over centuries. Classic example: Mount St. Helens after the 1980 eruption — primary succession on lava flows is just beginning to support grasses 45 years later. Glacier Bay AK (William Cooper's classic 1923 study; Cooper retraced glacial retreat from 1794 onward) shows ~200 years from bare rock to spruce-hemlock climax forest. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 2.7) require students to distinguish from secondary succession and explain the slow timescale of soil formation (~1 cm per 500-1000 yr).

Secondary succession occurs on disturbed land where soil remains intact — after forest fire, clearcut, abandoned farm field, or hurricane. Faster than primary succession (decades vs centuries) because seed bank, root systems, and nutrients persist. Classic example: New England forests regrowing on abandoned 19th-century farms (now ~80% forested again per Thoreau's Walden journals). After the 1988 Yellowstone fires (793,000 acres burned), lodgepole pine regrew from serotinous cones that require fire heat to open — a fire-adapted species. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 2.7) require students to trace stages (annual weeds to perennials to shrubs to early-successional trees like aspen and birch to climax forest like oak-hickory).

Pioneer species are the first organisms to colonize lifeless substrate during primary succession. They tolerate extreme conditions (high UV, no soil, low nutrients) and modify the environment for later colonizers — a process called facilitation (Clements 1916). Classic pioneers: crustose lichens (a mutualism of fungi + cyanobacteria/algae) physically and chemically weather rock, building primitive soil. Mosses follow, retaining moisture. Both fix atmospheric N. Then grasses, herbs, shrubs, then trees. On Mount St. Helens post-1980, lupines (Lupinus lepidus) emerged as key pioneers — N-fixing legumes that enriched volcanic ash. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 2.7) require students to name lichens specifically and explain N-fixation's role.

A climax community is the stable, self-replicating end-stage of succession dominated by long-lived species suited to local climate — oak-hickory in eastern US, spruce-fir in boreal Canada, redwood in Pacific NW. Frederic Clements (1916) proposed climax as a single deterministic endpoint. Modern view (Henry Gleason, A.G. Tansley): multiple stable states are possible (alternative stable states; Holling resilience theory). Climate change is shifting climax composition — tree species ranges shifting poleward at ~17 km/decade (Chen et al. 2011 Science). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 2.7) require students to predict climax communities from regional climate and explain why fire suppression alters climax composition (e.g., longleaf pine to hardwoods in southeast US).

questions.prompt: Range of conditions species can survive.
questions.explanation: Tolerance range is the span of an abiotic factor (temperature, pH, salinity, oxygen) within which a species can survive and reproduce. Within this range is an optimal zone (peak fitness), flanked by zones of physiological stress where survival is possible but growth/reproduction is impaired, and beyond which intolerance causes death. Victor Shelford (1913) formalized this as the Law of Tolerance. Stenotopic species have narrow tolerance (specialists like trout in cold streams); eurytopic species have broad tolerance (generalists like raccoons, crows). Climate change is shifting species ranges poleward and uphill as tolerance ranges are exceeded. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 2.8) require students to predict range shifts from climate data.

A limiting factor is the single resource or condition most restricting growth, productivity, or population size — even when all other resources are abundant. Justus von Liebig (1840) articulated this as the Law of the Minimum: a plant's yield is limited by the nutrient in scarcest supply (often N or P in terrestrial systems; iron in ocean phytoplankton — John Martin's 1988 iron-hypothesis IronEx experiments). Adding the limiting factor boosts productivity; adding non-limiting nutrients does nothing until the limiter shifts. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 2.8, 8.6) chain limiting factors with eutrophication — adding limiting P to a lake triggers algal blooms, but only if P was indeed limiting.

The optimum range is the narrow span of abiotic conditions where a species achieves peak growth, survival, and reproduction. Within Shelford's tolerance curve (1913), the optimum is the peak of a bell-shaped fitness function. Trout thrive at 10-15 degC; coral at 23-29 degC with stable salinity; corn at 25-30 degC with adequate moisture. Outside optimum, organisms enter zones of physiological stress. Climate change pushes ecosystems outside optimum: when sea-surface temperatures exceed coral optimum by >1-2 degC for weeks, mass bleaching occurs (1998, 2010, 2014-17 events killed 30% of Great Barrier Reef). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 2.8) require students to predict consequences of departing optimum conditions.

questions.prompt: Conditions species can survive but not thrive.
questions.explanation: Zones of physiological stress flank the optimum range on both sides — species survive here but with reduced growth, lower fecundity, and increased mortality. In Shelford's tolerance curve (1913), these are the shoulders of the bell. Trout stressed above 18 degC stop feeding and become vulnerable to disease (key concern in thermal pollution from power plants discharging warm water). Continued stress at the population level reduces competitive ability and eventually causes local extinction. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 2.8) require students to recognize stress zones as early-warning indicators of larger ecosystem change — declining bee colonies, amphibian die-offs, and coral paling all signal stress preceding mass mortality.

Zones of intolerance lie beyond the tolerance range — conditions where mortality exceeds reproduction and species cannot persist. Shelford's Law of Tolerance (1913). Examples: stratospheric ozone depletion increases UV-B exposure beyond amphibian eggs' tolerance, causing developmental failure (Joseph Kiesecker and Andrew Blaustein 1990s research on Cascade frogs). Ocean acidification (pH dropping from 8.2 toward 7.8 by 2100) exceeds many calcifier species' tolerance, dissolving pteropod shells in the Southern Ocean. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 2.8, 9.6) require students to predict species range contractions and local extinctions when environmental change pushes conditions past tolerance limits.

An adaptation is a heritable trait that enhances survival or reproduction in a particular environment, evolved through natural selection (Charles Darwin, 'On the Origin of Species,' 1859). Adaptations can be structural (camel hump, polar bear fur), physiological (kangaroo rat producing concentrated urine, antifreeze proteins in Antarctic fish), or behavioral (bird migration, hibernation). Adaptations are NOT designed intentionally — they accumulate from random mutations that prove advantageous. Climate change tests adaptive capacity: species with long generation times (oaks, whales) cannot adapt fast enough to climate shift. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 2.6) require students to identify specific adaptations and predict mismatches under rapid environmental change.

Xerophytes are plants adapted to arid environments through structural and physiological modifications: thick waxy cuticles, reduced/spiny leaves, deep taproots (mesquite roots reach 50+ m), water-storing succulent tissues (cacti, agaves), CAM photosynthesis (open stomata only at night, reducing transpiration ~90% vs C3 plants). The saguaro cactus (Carnegiea gigantea) can store ~5,000 L of water and live 200+ years. CAM photosynthesis was discovered in stonecrop plants and named for its presence in Crassulaceae. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 1.1, 2.6) require students to connect xerophyte adaptations to desert biome conditions and predict shifts as climate change expands arid regions.

Hydrophytes are plants adapted to aquatic or saturated environments: aerenchyma (air-conducting spongy tissue moving O2 from leaves to submerged roots, since waterlogged soils are anoxic), reduced or absent cuticles (water absorption through leaves), flexible stems that bend with currents, floating leaves with stomata only on upper surface (Nymphaea water lilies). Mangroves are specialized hydrophytes with pneumatophores (aerial roots), salt-excretion glands, and viviparous seedlings. Loss of mangroves (~35% globally since 1980, FAO data) eliminates storm protection, nurseries for fisheries, and major carbon sinks ('blue carbon'). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 1.1, 2.6) require students to connect hydrophyte traits to wetland services.

Cold tolerance adaptations include thick insulating fur (polar bears, musk oxen), subcutaneous blubber (whales, seals — 30-50% body fat), counter-current heat exchange in extremities (penguin feet), antifreeze glycoproteins in Antarctic icefish (Notothenioidei, discovered by Arthur DeVries 1969 — lower blood freezing point), hibernation, migration, and supercooling in insects. Bergmann's rule (1847): within a species, populations in colder climates tend to be larger (lower surface-to-volume ratio reduces heat loss). Climate change disrupts these adaptations: warming forces polar bears to spend more time on land as sea ice declines (Arctic summer ice has dropped ~40% since 1979 per NSIDC). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 2.6, 9.6) require students to predict climate-driven range shifts.

Camouflage (crypsis) is coloration or patterning that conceals an organism from predators (or helps predators ambush prey). The peppered moth (Biston betularia) is the textbook example of natural selection: during 19th-century industrial soot pollution in Manchester, melanic (dark) forms rose from <1% to >95% as light forms became visible on darkened tree bark to predatory birds (J.B.S. Haldane, then H.B.D. Kettlewell's 1955 experiments). After Clean Air Acts (UK 1956, US 1970) reduced soot, light forms recovered — a measurable evolutionary response to pollution. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 2.6, 7.5) connect industrial-era selection pressures, the Clean Air Act of 1970, and adaptive evolution measurable in human timescales.

Exponential growth: dN/dt = rN, where N = population size, r = intrinsic growth rate (births minus deaths per capita). Produces a J-shaped curve — population doubles in fixed time (doubling time T = ln(2)/r ~ 0.693/r). Thomas Malthus (1798, 'An Essay on the Principle of Population') warned of human exponential growth outstripping arithmetic food increases. Bacterial growth in unlimited media, invasive species without predators (rabbits in Australia), and human population from 1804 (1 billion) to 2022 (8 billion) all illustrate exponential patterns. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 3.4) require students to calculate doubling times and predict when exponential growth must transition to logistic as resources become limiting.

Logistic growth: dN/dt = rN(1 - N/K), where K is carrying capacity. Produces an S-shaped (sigmoidal) curve — growth accelerates initially, then decelerates as N approaches K, leveling at equilibrium. Pierre-Francois Verhulst derived the equation in 1838; Raymond Pearl rediscovered it in 1920 fitting to Drosophila populations. Most real populations show logistic patterns: yeast in flasks, Paramecium in Gause's 1934 experiments, harbor seals in Alaska after hunting bans. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 3.4) require students to compute population growth at various N/K ratios and predict when growth is fastest (at N = K/2).

Carrying capacity (K) is the maximum population size an environment can sustainably support, set by limiting resources (food, water, space, nesting sites). K is not fixed — it varies with seasonal resource availability and ecosystem degradation. Garrett Hardin's 'Tragedy of the Commons' (Science 1968) showed that unregulated access to common resources drives population overshoot above K, then crash. The Anasazi at Chaco Canyon (collapse ~AD 1150) and the Norse colonies in Greenland (extinct ~1400) illustrate human populations exceeding local K. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 3.4, 3.7) require students to calculate K from population trajectories and predict consequences of K reduction from habitat loss.

Overshoot occurs when a population exceeds carrying capacity (N > K) — often due to delayed feedbacks (resources don't appear scarce until exhausted) or temporary windfall conditions. The population then experiences resource depletion, increased mortality, and dieback. Classic case: 29 reindeer introduced to St. Matthew Island, Alaska (1944) grew to 6,000 by 1963 then crashed to 42 by 1966 — a 99% mortality event (David Klein 1968 documentation). Easter Island deforestation and population collapse around 1600 is another. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 3.4, 3.7) require students to predict overshoot consequences, including soil degradation, biodiversity loss, and the lower K that often results after dieback.

Dieback (population crash) is the rapid mortality following overshoot, returning population below K — often well below original K due to permanent habitat damage from over-exploitation. Crash trajectories vary: gradual decline with starvation/disease, or rapid mortality from a triggering event. Passenger pigeons numbered ~3-5 billion in 1850s North America (largest bird species ever recorded) and were hunted to extinction by 1914 (Martha, last bird, died at Cincinnati Zoo Sept 1, 1914). Atlantic cod stocks collapsed 99% off Newfoundland by 1992 from overfishing, never recovered despite moratorium. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 3.4, 5.10) require students to argue why post-dieback K is often lower than pre-dieback K.

questions.prompt: Species with many offspring, little care.
questions.explanation: r-selected species pursue a 'fast-and-many' reproductive strategy: many small offspring, short lifespans, early reproduction, little parental care, high intrinsic growth rate (r). Robert MacArthur and E.O. Wilson formalized r/K selection theory in 'The Theory of Island Biogeography' (1967). Examples: insects (housefly produces 500+ eggs), rodents, weedy plants (dandelions), most fish. r-strategists boom under disturbance and exploit ephemeral resources — they are often pioneer species in succession and invasive species. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 3.5) require students to predict population behavior — r-strategists experience boom-bust cycles, are density-independent, and respond rapidly to environmental change.

questions.prompt: Species with few offspring, much care.
questions.explanation: K-selected species pursue a 'slow-and-few' strategy: few large offspring, long lifespans, delayed reproduction, intensive parental care, low r but high competitive ability near K. MacArthur and Wilson (1967). Examples: elephants (one calf every 4-5 years; 70-year lifespan), whales, primates including humans, oak trees, condors. K-strategists thrive in stable mature ecosystems but recover slowly from disturbance — they are disproportionately represented on endangered species lists. The IUCN Red List shows K-strategists like rhinos, tigers, and great apes are most vulnerable to extinction. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 3.5, 9.7) chain K-selection with conservation: slow recovery means K-strategists need active intervention.

Intrinsic rate of increase (r) is the per-capita growth rate of a population under ideal conditions (unlimited resources, no predation, no disease). r = b - d where b = birth rate and d = death rate (per individual per unit time). r-selected species have high r (>0.5/yr); K-selected species have low r (<0.05/yr). Doubling time T = ln(2)/r ~ 0.693/r — a population growing at r = 0.02 (2%/yr, like contemporary human growth) doubles every ~35 years. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 3.4, 3.5) require students to compute r from b and d data and connect r to reproductive strategy and successional/invasive potential.

questions.prompt: K equals stable population near limit.
questions.explanation: Carrying capacity (K) is the equilibrium population size — at K, birth and death rates balance, dN/dt = 0. K depends on limiting resources (food, water, nesting sites) and can shift with environmental change. Human carrying capacity estimates range widely from 8 to 16 billion depending on consumption patterns and technology (Joel Cohen's 'How Many People Can the Earth Support?' 1995). The Ecological Footprint (Mathis Wackernagel and William Rees 1996) shows humanity is currently using ~1.7 Earths worth of biocapacity — overshoot of global K. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 3.4, 3.7) require students to argue that K is dynamic and connect to ecological footprint analysis.

A survivorship curve plots the percentage of an original cohort surviving versus age (Raymond Pearl 1928). Three classic types: Type I (low juvenile mortality, high old-age mortality — K-selected: humans, elephants, large mammals); Type II (constant mortality across ages — birds, small mammals, hydra); Type III (very high juvenile mortality, low adult mortality — r-selected: fish, oysters, oak trees from acorn). The three-curve framework was formalized by Robert Slobodkin (1961). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 3.5) require students to identify curve type from data and connect curve shape to reproductive strategy — Type I species produce few young with high parental investment; Type III species produce vast numbers with no care.

A population pyramid (age structure diagram) plots male and female population by 5-year age cohorts. Shape predicts future growth: expansive (wide base, like Nigeria, Niger) indicates rapid growth with many young; stationary (rectangular, like Denmark, France) indicates stable replacement-level fertility; constrictive (narrow base, like Japan, Italy, Germany) indicates declining population with aging society. Population momentum: even if fertility drops to replacement immediately, expansive pyramids grow for ~50 years as large youth cohorts reach reproductive age. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 3.6) require students to interpret pyramid shape, predict 30-year trajectories, and connect to dependency ratios (youth + elderly) / working age.

questions.prompt: Birth rate minus death rate per 1000.
questions.explanation: Rate of natural increase (RNI) = (crude birth rate - crude death rate)/10, expressed as percent/year. Crude birth rate (CBR) and crude death rate (CDR) are deaths/births per 1,000 population. Doubling time = 70/r% (Rule of 70 — approximation of ln(2)/r * 100). Global RNI peaked at 2.1% in 1968 (Paul Ehrlich's 'The Population Bomb' year, predicting mass famine) and declined to 0.8% by 2024. Niger's RNI is 3.7% (doubling time ~19 years); Japan's is -0.3% (declining). Migration is excluded from RNI but added for total growth. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 3.6) require RNI calculation from CBR/CDR data and doubling-time application.

Total fertility rate (TFR) is the average number of children a woman would have over her lifetime at current age-specific fertility rates. Replacement-level fertility is ~2.1 (slightly above 2 to account for childhood mortality). Above 2.1 = population grows; below 2.1 = eventual decline (after population momentum dissipates). Global TFR fell from 5 in 1950 to 2.3 in 2024 (UN World Population Prospects). Niger has TFR ~6.8; South Korea ~0.78 (lowest globally). Driver: female education — every additional year of girls' schooling reduces fertility ~0.3-0.5. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 3.6) require students to predict TFR consequences and argue education is the most effective demographic intervention.

Urbanization is the demographic shift from rural to urban living, driven by economic opportunity and improved services. In 1800, 3% of humans lived in cities; in 1900, 14%; in 2008 humanity crossed 50% urban (UN-Habitat data); by 2050, 68% projected. Megacities (10+ million): Tokyo (37M), Delhi (32M), Shanghai (28M), Sao Paulo (22M). Urbanization brings benefits (lower per-capita emissions, better access to education/healthcare, lower fertility) and costs (slum formation — ~1 billion in informal settlements per UN; heat-island effect raising temperatures 2-5 degC; food/water/waste pressures). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 5.16) require students to argue environmental tradeoffs of urbanization.

The Demographic Transition Model (Warren Thompson 1929, refined by Frank Notestein 1945) describes population change in four (sometimes five) stages as societies develop: Stage 1 (pre-industrial: high CBR + high CDR, stable population); Stage 2 (early industrial: CDR drops via medicine/sanitation, CBR still high, rapid growth); Stage 3 (industrial: CBR drops as women's education and urbanization rise, growth slows); Stage 4 (post-industrial: CBR and CDR both low, stable); Stage 5 (proposed: CBR below replacement, decline — Japan, S. Korea, Italy). Every developed nation has passed through this transition. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 3.6, 3.8) require students to place countries in stages from CBR/CDR data.

Quadrat sampling estimates population density of sessile or slow-moving species by counting individuals in randomly placed sample plots (quadrats, typically 1 m^2) and extrapolating to total area. Frederick Clements pioneered quadrat methods in plant ecology around 1900. Total population estimate = (count per quadrat) x (total area / quadrat area). Used for trees, wildflowers, intertidal organisms, soil arthropods. Random placement avoids bias; systematic grid placement captures spatial heterogeneity. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 3.3) frequently provide quadrat data and require students to calculate density and extrapolate population — also testing precision via replication and sample-size calculations.

Mark-recapture (Lincoln-Petersen method) estimates mobile-animal populations: capture, mark, and release a sample (M); after mixing, capture a second sample (C); count marked individuals in second sample (R). Total population N = (M x C)/R. Frederick Lincoln applied it to waterfowl in 1930; C.G.J. Petersen had earlier applied it to fish in 1896. Assumes closed population, equal catchability, no mark loss. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 3.3) frequently provide M, C, R data and require N calculation. Example: 100 fish marked, 80 recaptured, 20 marked among recaptures gives N = (100 x 80)/20 = 400 fish.

The Lincoln-Petersen estimator is N = M*C/R where M = total individuals marked and released, C = total in recapture sample, R = marked individuals in recapture sample. The ratio M/N (proportion of population marked) equals R/C (proportion marked in recapture) — basic probability. Lincoln (1930 waterfowl), Petersen (1896 plaice fish). Assumes the marked individuals fully mix back into the population, no births or deaths during the study, and equal catchability. Violations bias estimates downward (if marked animals are easier to recapture, R is too high, N too low). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 3.3) require N calculation and identification of assumption violations from scenarios.

Sign counting estimates populations of cryptic or rare species (mountain lions, wolves, bears, owls) by counting indirect evidence: tracks, scat, dens, browse damage, scrapes, vocalizations. Less precise than direct counts but feasible for elusive animals. Calibration via mark-recapture or DNA from scat (non-invasive genetic sampling). Wolf monitoring in Yellowstone uses scat DNA to identify individuals. Snow tracking helps Canada lynx and wolverine surveys. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 3.3) require students to argue when sign counting is preferable to direct sampling — for example, when the species is too rare, too elusive, or too dangerous for direct observation, or when habitat (dense forest) prevents visual counts.

questions.prompt: Population at risk of extinction count.
questions.explanation: Minimum Viable Population (MVP) is the smallest number of individuals required for a population to persist with a high probability (e.g., 95% chance of survival over 100+ years) given environmental and genetic stochasticity. Mark Shaffer coined the term in 1981. Below MVP, inbreeding depression, genetic drift, and demographic randomness drive extinction. The classic '50/500 rule' (Ian Franklin 1980; Russell Lande 1988): 50 individuals to avoid short-term inbreeding, 500 to maintain long-term evolutionary potential. Modern Population Viability Analysis (PVA) models give species-specific MVPs. Florida panther MVP analysis prompted 1995 Texas cougar introductions. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.7) connect MVP to Endangered Species Act protection.

questions.prompt: Factor affecting population proportional to density.
questions.explanation: Density-dependent factors regulate population growth proportionally to population density — their impact intensifies as N rises and relaxes as N falls. Examples: disease transmission (denser populations transmit faster; the 1918 flu pandemic spread fastest in crowded WWI trenches and ports), competition for food and mates, accumulation of toxic waste, predation efficiency. Density-dependent factors stabilize populations near K — the regulatory mechanism in the logistic equation. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 3.7) require students to distinguish density-dependent (regulating) from density-independent (perturbing) and predict which population pressures intensify near K (mostly density-dependent — disease, food shortage, social stress).

questions.prompt: Factor affecting population regardless of density.
questions.explanation: Density-independent factors affect populations regardless of size: severe weather (drought, hurricanes), wildfires, volcanic eruptions, and certain forms of human disturbance (deforestation, oil spills). The Mount St. Helens eruption (1980) killed wildlife at all densities indiscriminately. Climate change is intensifying density-independent mortality through increased extreme weather frequency. Density-independent factors push populations away from equilibrium but do not regulate them — they cause boom-bust cycles especially in r-strategists like insects (whose populations swing with temperature/moisture). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 3.7) require students to distinguish density-independent from density-dependent and predict which factors will intensify under climate change.

questions.prompt: Competition for same niche within species.
questions.explanation: Intraspecific competition occurs among individuals of the same species for identical limiting resources (food, water, territory, mates). Because conspecifics have identical niches, competition is more intense than interspecific competition. Drives self-thinning in plant populations (Yoda's -3/2 power law, 1963) — dense forest stands self-thin from seedling to mature tree over decades. In animals, intraspecific competition drives dispersal, territoriality (red-tailed hawks defend exclusive territories), and dominance hierarchies (wolves). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 3.7) require students to contrast intraspecific (density-dependent regulator within species) with interspecific competition (between species — drives competitive exclusion per Gause 1934).

Interspecific competition occurs between different species for shared limiting resources. Gause's competitive exclusion principle (1934 Paramecium experiments): two species cannot coexist on identical resources — one must outcompete and exclude the other, or shift niche (resource partitioning, Robert MacArthur's 1958 study of five Dendroica warbler species partitioning spruce trees). Invasive species often outcompete natives because they lack co-evolved predators and pathogens. Zebra mussels (Dreissena polymorpha, since 1988 Great Lakes) outcompete native unionid mussels by filtering more efficiently. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 2.3, 9.6) require students to predict competitive outcomes from niche overlap data.

questions.prompt: Number of individuals per unit area.
questions.explanation: Population density is the number of individuals per unit area (terrestrial) or volume (aquatic). Density affects intraspecific competition, disease transmission, mating success, and behavior. Density distributions vary spatially: clumped (most common — fish schools, herd animals), uniform (territorial — penguin colonies, desert shrubs spaced by water competition), or random (rare in nature). Density-dependent factors intensify as density rises, pushing populations toward K. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 3.1, 3.7) require students to calculate density from data and predict effects: high density triggers stress hormones (cortisol) in mammals, reducing fecundity — a self-regulating mechanism documented in lemming and house-mouse cycles.

Plate tectonics theory holds that Earth's lithosphere is divided into ~15 major plates that move ~2-10 cm/yr atop the convecting asthenosphere. Alfred Wegener proposed continental drift in 1912 (matching fossils, rock types, coastlines) but lacked a mechanism. Mid-ocean-ridge mapping in the 1950s and Harry Hess's seafloor spreading hypothesis (1962) supplied the mechanism. Confirmed by paleomagnetic stripes (Vine and Matthews 1963). Plate motion drives earthquakes, volcanoes, mountain building, and shapes climate over millions of years (closure of Isthmus of Panama ~3 Ma triggered Pleistocene glaciations). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 4.1) require students to predict geologic hazards from plate-boundary type.

Divergent boundaries are where plates move apart and new crust forms. At mid-ocean ridges (Mid-Atlantic Ridge, East Pacific Rise) magma rises, cools, and creates new oceanic lithosphere — Harry Hess's seafloor spreading (1962). Iceland straddles the Mid-Atlantic Ridge and is growing ~2 cm/yr; volcanic activity (Eyjafjallajokull 2010 ash cloud disrupted European air travel) is concentrated along the rift. On land, divergent boundaries create rift valleys (East African Rift, where humanity evolved; if it continues to spread, the Horn of Africa will separate). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 4.1) require students to identify divergent boundaries and connect to volcanic and seismic hazards.

Convergent boundaries are where plates collide. Three types based on plate density: oceanic-continental (denser ocean subducts under continent forming volcanoes — Andes from Nazca plate subducting under South American plate); oceanic-oceanic (one ocean plate subducts, forming volcanic island arcs — Japan, Philippines, Aleutians); continental-continental (no subduction, plates pile up forming mountains — Himalayas from Indian plate colliding with Eurasian plate ~50 Ma ago, still rising ~5 mm/yr). The Pacific Ring of Fire (75% of world volcanoes, 90% of earthquakes) traces convergent boundaries. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 4.1) require students to predict hazards (earthquakes, volcanoes, tsunamis) from boundary type.

Transform boundaries are where plates slide horizontally past each other; no new crust forms and no subduction occurs, but immense earthquakes result from stress accumulation and sudden release. The San Andreas Fault (California; Pacific plate moves NW past North American plate at ~5 cm/yr) is the textbook example. The 1906 San Francisco earthquake (M 7.9) killed 3,000 and triggered fires that destroyed the city — a turning point in seismic engineering. Anatolian Fault (Turkey) produces devastating earthquakes (1999 Izmit M 7.6 killed 17,000; 2023 Kahramanmaras M 7.8 killed 60,000+). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 4.1) require students to identify transform boundaries and connect to seismic-hazard mapping.

Subduction zones are where denser oceanic plates dive beneath less-dense continental or oceanic plates at convergent boundaries, returning to the mantle. Marked by deep ocean trenches (Marianas Trench, 11 km deep — deepest spot on Earth) and Wadati-Benioff zones of progressively deeper earthquakes. Subducting plates melt and feed volcanic arcs above (Cascade Mountains: Mount St. Helens 1980 eruption from Juan de Fuca plate subducting). Subduction zones produce the largest earthquakes (M 9+: Sumatra 2004 M 9.1 with 230,000+ tsunami deaths; Tohoku Japan 2011 M 9.1 with Fukushima reactor failure). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 4.1) require students to predict tsunami risk from subduction-zone earthquakes.

Topsoil (A horizon) is the upper mineral soil layer where organic matter mixes with minerals — dark, nutrient-rich, and biologically active with bacteria, fungi, earthworms, and arthropods. It supports most root growth and plant productivity. Topsoil forms slowly (~1 cm per 500-1000 years from rock weathering and organic accumulation) — a non-renewable resource on human timescales. The Dust Bowl (1930s) eroded ~75 million acres of US Great Plains topsoil from deep plowing of native grassland; Hugh Hammond Bennett's testimony led to the 1935 Soil Conservation Act. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 4.4, 5.2) require students to argue why topsoil loss is largely irreversible and connect to sustainable agriculture (cover cropping, no-till).

The O (organic) horizon is the topmost soil layer composed primarily of decomposing leaf litter, dead roots, fungal hyphae, and humus — partially decomposed organic matter that holds nutrients and water. Most pronounced in forest soils where leaf fall accumulates. Provides nutrients (N, P, K) and improves soil structure, water retention, and microbial activity. Tropical rainforests have surprisingly thin O horizons (nutrients are locked in living biomass, decomposition is rapid) — when cleared for agriculture, soils become quickly infertile. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 4.4) require students to identify O horizon and connect to soil productivity, composting, and biogeochemical cycling.

The B horizon (subsoil) is the zone of mineral accumulation, where clay, iron oxides, and minerals leached from the A horizon (eluviation) accumulate (illuviation). Often reddish or yellowish from iron-oxide minerals. Holds plant nutrients but lower in organic matter than the A horizon. Tropical lateritic soils have B horizons rich in iron and aluminum oxides (often forming bauxite for aluminum mining). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 4.4) require students to sequence soil horizons (O-A-B-C-R from surface to bedrock) and explain leaching: water percolating downward carries dissolved minerals from A to B horizon, depleting topsoil over time — accelerated by industrial agriculture without cover crops.

The C horizon consists of weathered parent material (regolith) — partially broken-down bedrock that has not yet developed soil structure. Lies directly above the R horizon (unweathered bedrock). The C horizon's parent material determines soil chemistry: limestone-derived soils tend alkaline (favoring most crops); granite-derived soils tend acidic (favoring conifers, blueberries). Soil weathering proceeds top-down — physical, chemical, and biological breakdown of bedrock to clay-sized particles, slowly developing into B and eventually A horizons over centuries to millennia. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 4.4) require students to sequence O-A-B-C-R horizons and connect to soil formation rates (~1 cm per 500-1000 years).

The soil texture triangle (USDA) classifies soils into 12 textural classes based on relative percentages of sand (0.05-2 mm), silt (0.002-0.05 mm), and clay (<0.002 mm) particles. Loam (~40% sand, 40% silt, 20% clay) is ideal for agriculture — good water retention, drainage, aeration, and nutrient holding. Sandy soils drain fast but hold little water/nutrients (poor for most crops, good for cacti). Clay soils hold water and nutrients but drain poorly and become compacted (good for rice paddies). Texture combined with structure determines porosity, permeability, and cation exchange capacity. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 4.4) require students to read the texture triangle and predict water/nutrient retention from percentages.

The troposphere is the lowest atmospheric layer, extending from surface to ~12 km (varies 8-17 km by latitude). Contains 75% of atmospheric mass and nearly all water vapor — site of essentially all weather. Temperature decreases with altitude at ~6.5 degC/km (environmental lapse rate). Tropopause caps the layer where temperature stops dropping. Greenhouse gases (CO2, CH4, H2O, N2O) trap outgoing infrared in the troposphere — the natural greenhouse effect raises Earth's mean surface temperature from ~-18 degC to +15 degC. Anthropogenic GHG accumulation amplifies this. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 4.2, 9.4) require students to identify the troposphere as the layer where tropospheric ozone (smog) forms and where most pollution accumulates.

The stratosphere extends from the tropopause (~12 km) to ~50 km altitude. Contains the ozone layer (15-35 km), where stratospheric O3 absorbs UV-B and UV-C radiation, protecting life from genetic damage and skin cancer. Temperature INCREASES with altitude in the stratosphere because UV absorption by O3 heats the air — opposite of the troposphere. CFCs released at the surface migrate upward (lifetime 50-100 years in the atmosphere) and destroy stratospheric O3 via chlorine-catalyzed reactions (Molina, Rowland, Crutzen — Nobel Prize 1995). The Antarctic ozone hole was discovered by Joe Farman 1985. The 1987 Montreal Protocol banned CFCs. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 4.2, 7.7) require students to distinguish stratospheric O3 (good) from tropospheric O3 (bad).

Nitrogen (N2) makes up 78.09% of the atmosphere by volume — the most abundant atmospheric gas. N2 is inert (triple bond, 945 kJ/mol bond energy) and biologically unavailable until 'fixed' to NH3 or NO3- by Rhizobium bacteria in legume root nodules, by lightning (~5% of natural fixation), or by the industrial Haber-Bosch process (Fritz Haber Nobel 1918) which now provides synthetic fertilizer feeding half of humanity. Nitrogen oxides (NOx) from combustion are pollutants forming acid rain and tropospheric ozone smog. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 4.2, 7.3) require students to identify N2 as the dominant gas and connect to the nitrogen cycle's biological-fixation step.

Oxygen (O2) makes up 20.95% of the atmosphere by volume — the second most abundant gas. Essential for aerobic respiration in nearly all multicellular life. O2 first accumulated in the atmosphere during the Great Oxidation Event ~2.4 billion years ago when cyanobacteria evolved photosynthesis, releasing O2 that oxidized iron in oceans (banded iron formations) before saturating the atmosphere. Modern O2 levels are maintained by photosynthesis (production) balanced by respiration and combustion (consumption). Tropospheric O2 is constant; stratospheric O3 (ozone) is a separate molecule. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 4.2) require students to identify O2 percentage and connect to dissolved oxygen in aquatic systems (BOD, eutrophication).

Ozone (O3) is a triatomic oxygen molecule that absorbs UV-B and UV-C radiation in the stratosphere (15-35 km altitude), shielding life from DNA damage, skin cancer, and cataracts. Stratospheric ozone formation: O2 + UV yields 2 O, then O + O2 yields O3 (Chapman cycle, Sydney Chapman 1930). CFCs released at the surface destroyed stratospheric ozone via chlorine-catalyzed reactions (one Cl atom destroys ~100,000 O3 molecules — Mario Molina, F. Sherwood Rowland, Paul Crutzen, Nobel Prize 1995). The 1987 Montreal Protocol phased out CFCs globally; ozone hole healing began ~2000 and is projected to fully recover by 2070. Tropospheric ozone (smog) is a pollutant. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 4.2, 7.7) test both.

Solar radiation (insolation = INcoming SOLar radiATION) is electromagnetic energy reaching Earth's atmosphere — averaging 1361 W/m^2 at the top of atmosphere (the solar constant). About 30% is reflected back to space (Earth's albedo); 70% is absorbed (50% by surface, 20% by atmosphere). Absorbed energy drives photosynthesis, weather, ocean circulation, the water cycle, and climate. Earth's energy budget is a closed system on geologic timescales — incoming solar must balance outgoing thermal radiation; anthropogenic GHGs disrupt this balance (~+1 W/m^2 forcing per IPCC AR6). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 4.6, 9.2) require students to apply energy-budget reasoning to climate change and renewable-energy capacity.

Earth's axial tilt of 23.5 degrees (relative to its orbital plane) causes seasons — not Earth-Sun distance (Earth is actually closest to Sun in January). When the Northern Hemisphere tilts toward the Sun (June solstice), it receives more concentrated sunlight (higher solar angle) and longer days, producing summer; the Southern Hemisphere experiences winter. Tilt also creates the Tropics of Cancer (23.5 N) and Capricorn (23.5 S) — the maximum latitudes the Sun reaches directly overhead. Milankovitch cycles (axial tilt varies 22.1-24.5 degrees over 41,000 years; orbital eccentricity 100,000 years; precession 26,000 years) drive ice ages. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 4.6) require students to explain seasonal variation from tilt.

At the June (summer) solstice (~June 21), Earth's Northern Hemisphere tilts maximally toward the Sun — the Sun appears directly overhead at the Tropic of Cancer (23.5 N). NH experiences longest day of year, most concentrated insolation, and warmest temperatures (peak temperature lags solstice ~1 month due to thermal inertia of oceans/land). The Southern Hemisphere simultaneously experiences winter solstice with shortest day. The equinoxes (Mar 21, Sep 23) occur when neither hemisphere tilts toward Sun — equal day/night globally. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 4.6) require students to connect solar geometry to seasonal energy budgets and biological phenology (growing season length).

Hadley cells are atmospheric circulation cells in tropical latitudes (0-30 N/S). George Hadley proposed them in 1735 to explain trade winds. Mechanism: solar heating near equator drives air upward, creating low pressure and convective rainfall (intertropical convergence zone, ITCZ); the rising air spreads poleward, cools, descends at ~30 N/S latitudes creating high pressure and arid conditions (Sahara, Sonoran, Atacama deserts), then flows back toward equator as trade winds. Coriolis effect deflects the surface winds westward (NE trade winds in NH, SE in SH). Climate change is expanding Hadley cells poleward, pushing desert zones outward (~0.5 degree latitude per decade). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 4.7) test Hadley-cell geography and impacts.

The Coriolis effect deflects moving objects (winds, ocean currents, missiles) due to Earth's rotation — to the right in the Northern Hemisphere, to the left in the Southern Hemisphere. Described mathematically by Gaspard-Gustave de Coriolis (1835). Strongest at poles, zero at equator. Drives the clockwise rotation of NH hurricanes/cyclones and counterclockwise rotation in SH. Combined with Hadley cells, Coriolis creates the NE trade winds (NH) and SE trade winds (SH). Affects ocean circulation: the great gyres of the North Atlantic and Pacific rotate clockwise; SH gyres counterclockwise. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 4.7) require students to predict storm rotation, wind direction, and ocean current direction from hemisphere.

questions.prompt: Water moving from surface to atmosphere.
questions.explanation: Evaporation is the phase change of water from liquid to gas, driven by solar energy. ~78% of evaporation occurs from ocean surfaces, ~22% from land (lakes, rivers, soil). Latent heat of vaporization (~2,260 kJ/kg) makes evaporation a major mechanism for energy transfer in the climate system — tropical evaporation moves vast heat poleward via atmospheric circulation. Clausius-Clapeyron relation: warmer air holds ~7% more water vapor per degC. Climate change intensifies the hydrologic cycle — more evaporation drives more extreme rainfall (heavier downpours, longer droughts). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 4.9) require students to label evaporation in cycle diagrams and predict climate-change intensification effects.

Transpiration is the release of water vapor from plant leaves through stomatal pores during photosynthesis — a large corn plant transpires ~200 L water in a growing season. Combined with surface evaporation, the process is called evapotranspiration (ET). Globally, transpiration accounts for ~40% of land-surface water flux to atmosphere. Tropical rainforests are particularly transpiration-intensive — the Amazon recycles its own rainfall through transpiration, generating ~30-50% of regional precipitation. Deforestation reduces transpiration, drying regional climate (Amazon dieback hypothesis). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 4.9) require students to label transpiration in cycle diagrams and connect to forest-climate feedback.

Precipitation is water falling from clouds to the surface — rain, snow, sleet, or hail. Forms when atmospheric water vapor cools below dewpoint, condenses around particles (cloud condensation nuclei: dust, sulfate aerosols, sea salt), and droplets coalesce until heavy enough to fall. Global average ~990 mm/yr land precipitation, but ranges from <50 mm (Atacama) to >11,000 mm (Mawsynram, India). Climate change intensifies precipitation extremes (Clausius-Clapeyron: +7% water vapor per degC warming = heavier downpours). Acid rain (pH<5.6) results when SO2/NOx combine with water vapor. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 4.9, 7.5) require students to predict precipitation patterns and acid-deposition impacts.

Infiltration is water soaking from surface into soil, where it can be absorbed by plant roots or percolate deeper to recharge aquifers. Rate depends on soil texture (sandy = fast, clay = slow), saturation, slope, and vegetation cover. Urbanization with impervious surfaces (concrete, asphalt) drastically reduces infiltration — Houston's 30% increase in impervious cover (1970-2010) intensified Hurricane Harvey 2017 flooding ($125 billion damages, 100+ deaths). Green infrastructure (rain gardens, permeable pavement, urban wetlands) restores infiltration. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 4.9, 5.15) require students to predict consequences of reduced infiltration: increased runoff, decreased groundwater recharge, and increased flood risk.

Runoff is precipitation flowing over the land surface (rather than infiltrating) into streams, rivers, and ultimately oceans. Increases with impervious surfaces (urban areas), saturated soils, steep slopes, and lack of vegetation. Carries nonpoint-source pollution: nitrogen and phosphorus from agricultural fertilizer (causing eutrophication and dead zones — the Gulf of Mexico dead zone reaches ~6,300 mi^2 from Mississippi River agricultural runoff), pesticides, sediment, oil, road salts, and pet waste. The 1972 Clean Water Act regulates point sources well but nonpoint sources remain difficult to control. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 4.9, 8.2) require students to predict runoff effects and propose mitigation (riparian buffers, contour plowing, terracing).

Industrial agriculture is the high-input, high-output production system using synthetic fertilizers, pesticides, mechanization, monocultures, irrigation, and concentrated animal feeding operations (CAFOs). Emerged post-WWII as Haber-Bosch nitrogen (Fritz Haber Nobel 1918) and the Green Revolution (Norman Borlaug Nobel Peace Prize 1970, wheat breeding) tripled global yields. Benefits: feeds 8 billion people; only ~10% of humans now farm vs ~90% historically. Costs: topsoil erosion (1 cm per 500-1000 yr to form, lost in years), aquifer depletion (Ogallala falling 1+ m/yr), N/P runoff causing dead zones (Gulf of Mexico ~6,300 mi^2), pesticide resistance, biodiversity loss. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 5.3) require students to argue trade-offs.

questions.prompt: Growing single crop on large scale.
questions.explanation: Monoculture is the practice of growing a single crop variety across large areas. Advantages: maximizes mechanization, simplifies management, uniform harvest. Disadvantages: genetic vulnerability to pest/disease (Irish potato famine 1845-49 killed ~1 million when one fungus, Phytophthora infestans, devastated lumper potato monoculture; banana industry threatened by Tropical Race 4 fungus attacking Cavendish monoculture, the same disease that destroyed Gros Michel bananas pre-1960), depleted soil nutrients (corn monocultures lose ~6 tons topsoil/acre/yr), high pesticide use. Polyculture and crop rotation reduce these risks. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 5.2, 5.3) require students to predict monoculture vulnerabilities and propose alternatives.

Polyculture grows multiple crop species together — reducing pest pressure, improving soil structure, and providing harvest insurance against weather/disease. The classic Mesoamerican 'Three Sisters' (corn, beans, squash) was developed by Indigenous peoples ~5,000 years ago: corn provides stalks for beans to climb; beans fix nitrogen for corn and squash; squash leaves shade the ground, conserving moisture and suppressing weeds. Modern polyculture systems include alley cropping, agroforestry, and integrated rice-fish cultivation. Yields per species are lower, but total per-acre productivity often exceeds monoculture. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 5.2, 5.4) require students to predict ecological benefits and explain why polyculture reduces synthetic-input dependence.

Organic farming excludes synthetic fertilizers, pesticides, GMOs, and antibiotics; relies on compost, crop rotation, biological pest control, and integrated pest management (IPM). Regulated in the US by the 1990 Organic Foods Production Act and USDA National Organic Program (NOP, 2002). Benefits: lower pollution, better soil health, higher biodiversity. Costs: ~20-25% lower yields per acre (Seufert et al. 2012 Nature meta-analysis), higher consumer prices. Global organic agriculture is ~1.6% of farmland but growing. Indian farmer Bhaskar Save and the broader rebellion against pesticide-heavy Green Revolution practices influenced organic principles. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 5.4) require trade-off analysis.

The Green Revolution (1940s-1970s) transformed global agriculture via high-yield dwarf wheat and rice varieties, synthetic fertilizers (Haber-Bosch N), pesticides, and irrigation. Norman Borlaug (Nobel Peace Prize 1970) bred semi-dwarf wheat at Mexico's CIMMYT — short stalks that don't collapse under heavy grain heads. Indian wheat yields doubled 1965-1972, averting predicted famines. M.S. Swaminathan led India's adoption. Costs: aquifer depletion, soil salinization, biodiversity loss, fertilizer runoff, pesticide resistance, displacement of subsistence farmers. Borlaug himself acknowledged the trade-offs. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 5.3) require students to weigh feeding 1+ billion against environmental and social costs.

Irrigation is the artificial application of water to crops. Globally, irrigation uses ~70% of all freshwater withdrawals and produces ~40% of food on 20% of cultivated land (FAO). Methods range from flood/furrow (10-40% efficient — most water lost to evaporation, runoff, deep percolation) to sprinklers (60-75%) to drip irrigation (90-95%). Major aquifers (Ogallala in US Great Plains, North China Plain, Punjab) are being depleted faster than recharge — non-renewable groundwater mining. The Aral Sea catastrophe: Soviet diversion of Amu Darya and Syr Darya rivers for cotton irrigation shrank the sea ~90% by 2014, salinizing the basin and collapsing fisheries. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 5.5) require students to weigh irrigation against sustainable water use.

Drip irrigation delivers water directly to plant roots through perforated tubing, achieving 90-95% efficiency vs 50-60% for sprinklers and 10-40% for flood irrigation. Developed in Israel by Simcha Blass in the 1950s; commercialized by Netafim in 1965. Saves 30-70% of water compared to traditional methods, reduces weed growth (water targeted to crops), enables fertigation (delivering soluble fertilizers with water), and reduces evaporation losses. Limitations: high initial cost, vulnerability to clogging from sediment/algae, less suitable for some crops (rice). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 5.5) require students to compare irrigation methods quantitatively and recommend drip for water-scarce regions.

Salinization is the accumulation of salts in soil from irrigation water containing dissolved minerals; evaporation leaves salts behind, eventually exceeding crop tolerance. Globally, ~20% of irrigated land is salinized (~62 million hectares), costing ~$27 billion/year in lost productivity (FAO). The Aral Sea Basin (Kazakhstan/Uzbekistan) is the worst case — salt-laden dust storms destroy regional ecosystems. California's Central Valley faces extensive salinization from Colorado River water. Solutions: drip irrigation, drainage tiles to remove salts, salt-tolerant crops (halophytes), fallow rotations, and gypsum amendments. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 5.5) require students to predict salinization from arid-region irrigation and propose mitigation.

questions.prompt: Water rising to surface in arid regions.
questions.explanation: Waterlogging occurs when over-irrigation or poor drainage saturates soil, raising the water table to the root zone.
