Roots suffocate from lack of O2 (most plants need >10% pore-space O2; waterlogged soil has <2%). Salinization frequently accompanies waterlogging — capillary action draws dissolved salts upward to the surface as water evaporates. Pakistan's Indus Basin is heavily affected — ~25% of irrigated land suffers waterlogging and salinization. Symptoms include yellow stunted plants, surface salt crusts ('white death'), and reduced yields. Solutions: drainage tiles, switching to drip irrigation, fallow rotations, and crop selection. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 5.5) require students to connect waterlogging to salinization.

Aquifer depletion (groundwater mining) occurs when extraction exceeds recharge — non-renewable groundwater 'mining.' The Ogallala Aquifer underlies 8 US Great Plains states (TX-SD) and irrigates ~20% of US cropland; water levels have fallen ~50 m in heavily pumped areas, with 30% of original water already gone. North China Plain aquifer drops ~1 m/yr; Punjab India ~0.5 m/yr; California Central Valley experiencing land subsidence of ~28 ft since 1925. Coastal aquifer depletion causes saltwater intrusion (Long Island, Bangladesh). Recharge takes thousands of years — Pleistocene-aged water. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 4.10, 5.5) require students to quantify depletion and propose sustainable-use limits.

Clearcutting removes all trees in a designated area, with the cleared land left for natural regeneration or replanting. Economic advantages: maximizes timber yield per dollar, simplifies harvesting and replanting. Environmental costs: massive soil erosion (5-100x baseline), nutrient leaching, habitat destruction, sediment-loading of streams, reduced biodiversity, increased flood risk. Pacific Northwest old-growth clearcutting in the 1980s-90s drove the spotted owl crisis and 1990 ESA listing. Clearcut sites lose ~1-3 cm topsoil per decade vs ~0.01 in undisturbed forest. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 5.6, 5.7) require students to predict ecological consequences and propose alternative silviculture (selective cutting, shelterwood).

Selective cutting (also called selection cutting) harvests only mature, marketable trees while leaving most of the forest standing. Maintains continuous forest cover, preserves wildlife habitat, reduces erosion, and supports natural regeneration. Cons: more expensive per board-foot (logging roads access fewer trees per trip), can damage residual trees, and 'high-grading' removes only the best trees, degrading remaining stock. The Menominee Forest in Wisconsin has been selectively harvested by the Menominee tribe since 1854 — sustainable yield with growing standing-volume for 170+ years. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 5.7) require students to contrast selective cutting with clearcutting and identify trade-offs of each.

Deforestation is the permanent removal of forest for agriculture, urbanization, mining, or grazing. Earth has lost ~30% of forest cover historically; current rate is ~10 million ha/year (FAO 2020), concentrated in tropical regions. Drivers: beef ranching (Amazon, 80% of deforestation), soy (Brazilian Cerrado), palm oil (Indonesia, Malaysia), and small-scale agriculture. Consequences: ~12-15% of global GHG emissions; biodiversity loss (E.O. Wilson estimated 27,000 species/year lost in 1990s tropical clearing); disrupted hydrologic cycle (Amazon recycles 30-50% of its own rainfall via transpiration); soil erosion. The 1992 Rio Earth Summit Forest Principles failed to halt loss. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 5.6, 9.1) require students to integrate causes and consequences.

Reforestation is replanting trees on land that was previously forested. Differs from afforestation (planting on land that wasn't recently forested). Globally, the Trillion Trees Initiative (2020) aims to restore 1 trillion trees by 2030. China's Three-North Shelter Forest Program (the 'Great Green Wall' since 1978) planted ~66 billion trees on 35 million hectares to slow Gobi Desert expansion. Costa Rica reversed deforestation — from 25% forest cover in 1985 to 60% today via Payments for Environmental Services. Concerns: tree-plantation monocultures (eucalyptus, pine) provide less biodiversity than native forest; planted trees must survive decades to bank carbon long-term. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 5.7, 9.10) require students to argue reforestation strategies.

Afforestation establishes forest on land that was not recently forested (grassland, desert, abandoned cropland) — contrasts with reforestation (replanting on previously forested land). Carbon-sequestration benefit: a mature tree absorbs ~22 kg CO2/yr. Examples: Iceland's century-long afforestation effort restoring forests lost to Viking-age sheep grazing; the Sahel 'Great Green Wall' (African Union 2007) aims to plant a 7,000 km tree belt across Africa to halt desertification. Caution: afforestation in grasslands can REDUCE albedo (darker tree cover absorbs more solar radiation than light grassland), partially offsetting carbon benefits, and disrupt native grassland biodiversity. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.10) require students to weigh trade-offs.

Surface (strip/open-pit) mining removes overburden (rock and soil above the deposit) to extract minerals or coal at the surface. Economic advantages: cheaper than underground mining, recovers ~90% of resource. Environmental costs: massive habitat destruction, acid mine drainage (AMD), water-table disruption, dust pollution, landscape scarring. The Bingham Canyon copper mine (Utah) is 4 km wide and 1.2 km deep — visible from space — and produces ~25% of US copper. Mountaintop removal in Appalachia has flattened 500+ peaks and buried 2,000+ miles of streams. The 1977 Surface Mining Control and Reclamation Act (SMCRA) requires post-mining land restoration. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 5.9) require students to predict ecological impacts.

Subsurface (underground) mining accesses deep deposits via vertical shafts and horizontal tunnels. More expensive per ton than surface mining (recovers ~50% of resource vs ~90%), but less surface disturbance. Significant occupational hazards: black lung (coal worker pneumoconiosis), silicosis, methane explosions (1907 Monongah West Virginia explosion killed 362, prompting US Bureau of Mines creation 1910), tunnel collapse (Chile's 2010 Copiapo mine rescue trapped 33 miners 700 m underground for 69 days). Land subsidence above abandoned tunnels (centralia Pennsylvania fire still burning since 1962) damages infrastructure. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 5.9) require students to compare surface vs subsurface trade-offs.

Mountaintop removal mining (MTR) blasts off Appalachian ridge tops to expose coal seams, dumping the rock spoil into adjacent valleys ('valley fills'). Since 1980, MTR has flattened 500+ peaks across West Virginia, Kentucky, Virginia, and Tennessee, buried >2,000 miles of headwater streams, and devastated headwater ecosystems. Studies link MTR to elevated cancer rates and birth defects in nearby communities (Hendryx and Ahern 2008). The Surface Mining Control and Reclamation Act (SMCRA 1977) requires reclamation but enforcement is contested. The Clean Water Act 1972 Section 404 governs valley fills. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 5.9) require students to predict downstream water-quality impacts.

Acid Mine Drainage (AMD) forms when pyrite (FeS2) and other sulfide minerals exposed by mining oxidize with water and air, generating sulfuric acid (H2SO4) and dissolved heavy metals (Fe, Al, Cu, Zn, Cd, Pb). Reaction: 4 FeS2 + 15 O2 + 14 H2O yields 4 Fe(OH)3 + 8 H2SO4. AMD lowers stream pH to 2-4 (battery-acid range), killing fish and aquatic insects. ~12,000 miles of US streams are AMD-impacted (EPA), concentrated in Appalachia. Mitigation: limestone trenches neutralize acid; constructed wetlands precipitate metals; passive treatment systems cost less long-term than active treatment. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 5.9, 8.2) require students to write the AMD chemistry and propose treatment.

Mine reclamation restores ecological function to land disturbed by mining: recontouring slopes, replacing topsoil, revegetating with native species, treating AMD, and managing water flows. Required under the Surface Mining Control and Reclamation Act (SMCRA 1977) — operators must post bonds and complete reclamation before bond release. Quality varies: some reclaimed sites return to wildlife habitat and pasture; many remain compacted, infertile 'reclamation-grade' grasslands. The Abandoned Mine Land (AML) Program funded by SMCRA fees has reclaimed thousands of pre-1977 sites. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 5.9) require students to evaluate reclamation success criteria and connect to mining permit/bond systems.

questions.prompt: Catching too many fish too fast.
questions.explanation: Overfishing occurs when catch exceeds the rate at which fish populations can replace themselves — exceeding maximum sustainable yield (MSY). FAO 2020 data: ~33% of global stocks overfished, ~60% at maximum biological limit. The Atlantic cod collapse off Newfoundland (1992) was a textbook case: a fishery that produced ~250,000 tons/yr for centuries declined 99% by 1992, prompting Canada's moratorium that put 30,000 fishers out of work — and the population still has not recovered 33 years later. Garrett Hardin's 'Tragedy of the Commons' (Science 1968) predicted this outcome for open-access fisheries. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 5.10) require students to identify causes and propose ITQs, MPAs, or other management.

Maximum Sustainable Yield (MSY) is the largest catch that can be taken from a fishery indefinitely without depleting the stock. From logistic-growth theory (Schaefer 1954 model), MSY occurs at half of carrying capacity (N = K/2) where the population growth rate is highest. Underlies the Magnuson-Stevens Fishery Conservation and Management Act (1976, amended 1996, 2007) which set MSY as the legal management target in US waters. Estimating MSY is difficult — overestimates lead to overfishing and collapse. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 5.10) require students to compute MSY = rK/4 from logistic-growth parameters and argue why precautionary buffers below MSY are needed.

Bycatch is the unintended capture of non-target species in fishing gear. Globally, ~40% of marine catch (38 million tons/yr) is bycatch (FAO 2009). Shrimp trawling has the worst ratio — ~5-20 kg of bycatch per kg of shrimp caught, including sea turtles. Turtle Excluder Devices (TEDs), required in US shrimp nets since 1987, reduce turtle bycatch ~97%. Long-line fishing for tuna and swordfish kills ~300,000 seabirds (albatross most affected) and 250,000 sea turtles annually. The Marine Mammal Protection Act (1972) banned 'dolphin-deadly' tuna fishing. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 5.10) require students to propose mitigation (TEDs, circle hooks, weighted lines).

Trawling drags large nets through water (midwater trawling) or along the seafloor (bottom trawling). Bottom trawling is especially destructive — it crushes and scrapes the seafloor like an underwater clearcut, destroying corals, sponges, and benthic communities. Globally, ~5 million km^2 of seafloor is trawled each year (~3x larger than annual deforested area). Cold-water corals are 1,000+ years old and cannot recover within human timescales after trawling damage. The European Union banned bottom trawling below 800 m in 2016 to protect deep-sea ecosystems. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 5.10) require students to predict ecological cascade effects and contrast with sustainable methods.

Aquaculture is the controlled cultivation of aquatic organisms — fish, shellfish, seaweed — in ponds, tanks, or net-pens. The fastest-growing food-production sector globally; aquaculture surpassed wild capture in 2014 and now provides ~50% of seafood consumed. Salmon, shrimp, tilapia, catfish are major species. Benefits: reduces pressure on wild stocks, controlled production. Costs: water pollution (waste nutrient discharge causes localized eutrophication), disease/parasite spread (sea lice from salmon farms infect wild salmon), antibiotic overuse, escapees diluting wild genetics, and feed pressure on wild forage fish (1 kg farmed salmon needs ~1-2 kg wild fishmeal — though improving). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 5.10) require students to weigh trade-offs.

questions.prompt: Most common fossil fuel for electricity.
questions.explanation: Coal is sedimentary rock formed from compressed Carboniferous-era (~360-300 Ma) plant matter. Globally generates ~35% of electricity (down from ~40% in 2010 as renewables and natural gas displace it); 5x higher CO2 emissions per unit energy than natural gas. Types by carbon content: lignite (25-35% C, lowest energy), bituminous (most common US grade, 45-86% C), anthracite (86-97% C, hottest burn). Burning releases CO2, SO2 (acid rain — Clean Air Act 1970 Title IV cap-and-trade reduced US SO2 ~92% by 2020), NOx, mercury, particulate matter, and radioactive trace elements. The Donora PA smog (1948) killed 20 and prompted early air-quality law. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 6.5, 7.5) require trade-off analysis.

Petroleum (crude oil) is liquid hydrocarbons formed from compressed marine organisms over millions of years, refined into gasoline, diesel, jet fuel, heating oil, lubricants, and petrochemicals (plastics, fertilizers). Powers ~95% of transportation worldwide. Top producers: US (Permian Basin, shale boom since 2008), Saudi Arabia, Russia. Major spills: Exxon Valdez 1989 (Alaska, 11 million gallons, devastated Prince William Sound) prompted Oil Pollution Act 1990 mandating double-hulled tankers; Deepwater Horizon 2010 (Gulf of Mexico, 134 million gallons, largest in US history, killed 11 workers) prompted SEMS safety reforms. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 6.6) integrate extraction, spill response, and sustainable-transport alternatives.

Natural gas (primarily CH4) is the cleanest-burning fossil fuel — emits ~50% less CO2 per unit energy than coal and no SO2 or mercury. Combustion: CH4 + 2 O2 yields CO2 + 2 H2O. Globally generates ~23% of electricity (rising as gas displaces coal). US production exploded post-2008 via hydraulic fracturing ('fracking') of Marcellus, Bakken, Permian shales — US became world's #1 producer by 2011. Costs: fugitive methane emissions during extraction (CH4 is ~80x more potent than CO2 over 20 years), groundwater contamination concerns, induced seismicity from wastewater injection (Oklahoma earthquakes rose from ~50/year to 900+ in 2015). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 6.7) require integrated trade-off analysis.

Shale gas is natural gas trapped in low-permeability shale formations, extracted via hydraulic fracturing (fracking). A horizontal well bore is drilled and high-pressure water (millions of gallons per well) + sand + chemicals fractures the shale, releasing trapped gas. The Marcellus Shale (PA/WV/NY/OH) is the second-largest gas field on Earth. US shale gas production tripled 2008-2020. Concerns: groundwater contamination (methane in drinking water near Dimock PA, documented in 'Gasland' film 2010), wastewater seismicity (Oklahoma swarm), surface footprint, fugitive methane (CH4 leakage ~2-4% offsets climate benefits), and chemical disclosure under Halliburton-loophole exemption from Safe Drinking Water Act. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 6.7) integrate trade-offs.

Tar (oil) sands are sandstone saturated with bitumen — extremely viscous, heavy oil that requires surface mining or in-situ steam injection (SAGD) for extraction. Alberta's Athabasca deposit holds 165 billion barrels (third-largest oil reserve globally). Environmental costs are severe: ~3x more CO2 per barrel than conventional oil (extraction + upgrading energy-intensive), boreal forest destruction (~700,000+ ha cleared), massive tailings ponds (200+ km^2, visible from space, leaking into Athabasca River), water consumption (2-4 barrels water per barrel oil). The Keystone XL pipeline (proposed 2008, cancelled 2021 by Biden) would have moved tar-sand oil to US refineries. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 6.6) require students to weigh energy security against environmental costs.

Nuclear fission splits heavy atomic nuclei (typically U-235 or Pu-239) into lighter fragments, releasing 200 MeV per fission event — roughly 50 million times more energy per atom than chemical combustion. Discovered by Otto Hahn, Lise Meitner, and Fritz Strassmann (1938). Controlled in nuclear reactors using moderators (water, graphite) and control rods (cadmium, boron) to absorb neutrons. Globally provides ~10% of electricity (~440 reactors in 32 countries). Zero CO2 emissions during operation. Concerns: nuclear waste (spent fuel radioactive for 240,000+ years), accident risk (Three Mile Island 1979, Chernobyl 1986, Fukushima 2011), weapons proliferation. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 6.10) demand trade-off analysis.

Nuclear fusion combines light nuclei (typically deuterium + tritium yielding helium + neutron) at temperatures >100 million degC, releasing 17.6 MeV per reaction. Powers the Sun (proton-proton chain) and all stars. Cleaner than fission — no long-lived radioactive waste, fuel from seawater (deuterium), inherently safe (cannot run away — stops if conditions are not maintained). Major experimental reactors: ITER (France, 35-nation project, started 2007, plasma 2025+) and Lawrence Livermore National Ignition Facility achieved net energy gain Dec 2022 (3.15 MJ out from 2.05 MJ laser input — historic milestone). Commercial fusion electricity likely still decades away. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 6.10) require students to compare fission vs fusion.

Uranium-235 is the primary fission fuel. Natural uranium is 99.3% U-238 (non-fissile) and only 0.7% U-235 (fissile). Reactor fuel is enriched to 3-5% U-235 (low-enriched uranium, LEU); weapons-grade is >90% (highly-enriched uranium, HEU). Enrichment via gas centrifuges or gaseous diffusion. Half-life: U-235 = 704 million years; U-238 = 4.5 billion years. Plutonium-239, bred from U-238 in reactors, is also fissile and used in some reactors and weapons. Major uranium producers: Kazakhstan, Australia, Canada. The Iran nuclear deal (JCPOA 2015, US withdrew 2018) limited Iranian enrichment to monitor proliferation risk. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 6.10) test enrichment vs weapons proliferation distinction.

Spent nuclear fuel contains fission products (Cs-137 half-life 30 yr, Sr-90 half-life 29 yr) and transuranic elements (Pu-239 half-life 24,100 yr) that remain dangerously radioactive for ~240,000 years. The US has ~90,000 metric tons of spent fuel stored at 80 sites awaiting permanent disposal. Yucca Mountain (NV) was designated as the US repository in 1987 but cancelled in 2010 amid political opposition. Finland's Onkalo (operational 2025) is the world's first deep geologic repository — copper canisters 450 m underground in granite. Reprocessing (France, UK, Japan) extracts Pu-239 for reuse but creates proliferation risk. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 6.10, 8.13) require students to weigh disposal options.

Chernobyl Reactor 4 (Pripyat, Ukrainian SSR) exploded April 26, 1986 during a flawed safety test — control-rod design flaw + operator errors caused steam explosion and graphite fire. Released ~5,300 PBq of radioactive material (Cs-137, I-131, Sr-90), contaminating Belarus, Ukraine, and Russia plus most of Europe via fallout. Immediate deaths: 31 (firefighters, operators); long-term cancer deaths: ~4,000 per WHO; up to ~93,000 per Greenpeace. The 30 km Exclusion Zone is largely abandoned (though wildlife has returned). The 'Sarcophagus' (1986) and New Safe Confinement (2016, $1.7 billion) contain the reactor. Compare with Three Mile Island 1979 (no fatalities, contained partial meltdown) and Fukushima 2011. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 6.10) integrate disasters with safety reforms.

Hydroelectric power generates electricity from moving water turning turbines — the oldest commercial renewable (Niagara Falls 1879). Globally provides ~16% of electricity (~60% of all renewables). Major dams: Three Gorges China (22.5 GW, world's largest), Itaipu Brazil/Paraguay (14 GW), Hoover US (2 GW, 1936). Benefits: zero CO2 during operation, flood control, water storage, peaking power. Costs: habitat fragmentation (blocks salmon migration — 4 lower Snake River dams reducing wild salmon ~95% since 1960s), greenhouse emissions from rotting biomass in reservoirs (especially tropical dams), displacement (Three Gorges displaced 1.3 million people), sediment trapping (Nile's Aswan High Dam 1970 starved downstream agriculture). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 6.4) demand trade-off analysis.

Solar photovoltaic (PV) panels convert sunlight directly to electricity via the photoelectric effect (Einstein Nobel 1921). Crystalline silicon PV is dominant (~95% market share); efficiency has risen from ~6% (Bell Labs 1954) to 22-24% (today's commercial panels), with multi-junction lab cells exceeding 47%. Costs collapsed from $76/W in 1977 to $0.20/W in 2024 (>99% decline). Global installed capacity reached ~1.6 TW in 2024 (~6% of global electricity). Benefits: zero operational emissions, modular scale (rooftop to utility), no water needs. Costs: intermittent (sun doesn't shine 24/7 — requires storage or backup), land use for utility-scale, panel-manufacturing chemicals (cadmium telluride), end-of-life recycling. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 6.11) integrate trade-offs.

Wind turbines convert kinetic energy of moving air into electricity (Betz limit caps efficiency at 59.3% — Albert Betz 1919). Modern turbines: 100-150 m hub heights, 80-170 m rotor diameters, 2-15 MW capacities. Globally provides ~7% of electricity; leading sources: China (largest installer), US (Texas dominant), EU (Germany, UK), Denmark (~50% of electricity from wind). Offshore wind (UK's Hornsea Wind Farm at 1.2 GW is world's largest) avoids land conflicts. Benefits: zero emissions, no water use. Costs: intermittency, visual impact, bird/bat mortality (~200,000-300,000 US bird deaths/yr — much less than cats at 1-4 billion or buildings at 600 million), low-frequency noise concerns. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 6.11) integrate trade-offs.

Geothermal energy taps Earth's internal heat (~99% of Earth's volume is above 1000 degC). Two main types: (1) high-grade in volcanic/tectonic-active regions — Iceland (geothermal supplies ~30% of electricity and ~90% of heating; The Geysers California 1.5 GW); (2) low-grade ground-source heat pumps for building HVAC (constant ~10-15 degC soil temperature). Benefits: 24/7 baseload (unlike sun/wind), low operational emissions, low land use. Costs: location-limited (best sites near plate boundaries), induced seismicity (Basel Switzerland 2006 project triggered M 3.4 earthquake, cancelled), gas releases (H2S, CO2), high upfront cost. Enhanced Geothermal Systems (EGS) drill into hot dry rock — potential for wider deployment. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 6.11) integrate trade-offs.

Biomass energy burns or processes plant/animal matter (wood, agricultural waste, corn ethanol, soybean biodiesel, manure). Provides ~10% of global energy (mostly traditional wood-burning for cooking — affects 2.4 billion people, causing 4 million premature deaths/yr from indoor air pollution per WHO). Carbon-neutral IF biomass regrowth equals combustion CO2 — controversial in practice. US Renewable Fuel Standard (2007) requires 36 billion gallons biofuels by 2022 (mostly corn ethanol). Corn ethanol's net energy ratio is barely positive (~1.3:1) and uses ~40% of US corn crop, raising food prices. Cellulosic biofuels (switchgrass, agricultural residue) have better energy balance but haven't scaled. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 6.9) demand life-cycle analysis.

questions.prompt: Ratio of useful to total energy.
questions.explanation: Energy efficiency = useful work output / total energy input, expressed as a percentage. Constrained by the Second Law of Thermodynamics — no real process reaches 100% (entropy always increases; some energy lost as low-grade heat). Examples: coal power plant ~35%, natural gas combined cycle ~62%, gasoline car ~20-25%, electric vehicle ~85%, incandescent bulb ~5%, LED bulb ~25%, residential heat pump ~300-400% (moves heat rather than creating it). The 'negawatt' (Amory Lovins, Rocky Mountain Institute) — energy saved through efficiency — is the cheapest energy resource. Global energy intensity (energy per GDP unit) has fallen ~1.5%/yr historically. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 6.13) require quantitative efficiency analysis.

Waste heat is the low-temperature thermal energy lost from energy conversion processes — a consequence of the Second Law of Thermodynamics. A coal power plant releases ~65% of fuel energy as waste heat (typically rejected via cooling towers or once-through cooling). Thermal pollution from power plants raises receiving-water temperatures, reducing dissolved oxygen and stressing aquatic life (regulated under Clean Water Act). Industrial waste heat can be captured for district heating (Denmark, Sweden — 60% of homes use district heating) or process steam, dramatically improving overall efficiency. Cogeneration (combined heat and power) captures ~70-85% of fuel energy vs ~35% for electricity alone. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 6.13) require students to identify and quantify thermodynamic losses.

Cogeneration (Combined Heat and Power, CHP) generates electricity AND captures waste heat for industrial process heat, space heating, or district heating — achieving overall fuel efficiency of 70-85% vs ~35% for electricity-only plants. Common at universities, hospitals, paper mills, refineries, and district-heating systems. Denmark and Finland get >50% of their heating from CHP district systems. Reduces CO2 emissions ~40% versus separate heat and electricity production. The US Department of Energy promotes CHP under the Industrial Assessment Centers program. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 6.13) require students to compute efficiency gains and predict CO2 reductions, connecting to industrial energy policy and climate-change mitigation strategies.

Energy conservation reduces consumption through behavior change (driving less, turning off lights) and/or efficiency upgrades (LEDs, insulation, heat pumps). 'Negawatts' (Amory Lovins) — saved energy — are the cheapest energy resource. Building energy use (~40% of US total) can drop 30-50% with retrofit insulation, high-efficiency HVAC, and LED lighting. California's energy efficiency programs since 1975 (under Art Rosenfeld's leadership) have kept per-capita electricity flat while the rest of the US grew ~50% — saving $74 billion. Demand-side management (utility programs paying customers to reduce peak use) is now widespread. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 6.14) require students to quantify savings and propose policy interventions.

ENERGY STAR is a voluntary US EPA/Department of Energy program (established 1992 under EPA Administrator William Reilly) that certifies energy-efficient products — appliances, electronics, buildings, homes. Products meeting strict efficiency criteria earn the blue ENERGY STAR label. Since 1992, ENERGY STAR has saved ~5 trillion kWh of electricity and avoided ~4 billion tons of CO2 emissions per EPA. ENERGY STAR refrigerators use ~9-10% less energy than baseline; certified buildings use ~35% less energy than typical. Other efficiency standards: SEER (air conditioner Seasonal Energy Efficiency Ratio), MPG (vehicle fuel economy), DOE appliance standards. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 6.13, 6.14) require students to argue voluntary vs mandatory efficiency policy.

questions.prompt: Goal of reducing fossil fuel use.
questions.explanation: Decarbonization is the systematic reduction of fossil fuels and CO2 emissions across the energy system, aiming for net-zero. Required by Paris Agreement (2015) to limit warming to 1.5-2 degC above pre-industrial. Major pathways: shift electricity to renewables + nuclear, electrify transportation (EVs replace ICE), electrify buildings (heat pumps replace gas furnaces), industrial process changes (hydrogen replacing coal in steel; CCS for cement), reduce demand via efficiency. Several countries have legally binding net-zero targets — UK (2050), Germany (2045), Sweden (2045), EU (2050), US (2050 — Inflation Reduction Act 2022 invests $369B in clean energy). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 6.11, 9.10) require students to evaluate policy effectiveness.

The Paris Agreement was adopted at COP21 (Conference of Parties to the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change) on December 12, 2015, in Paris, entering into force November 4, 2016. 196 parties pledged Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) to limit warming to 'well below 2 degC' above pre-industrial, with aspirational 1.5 degC. Bottom-up structure (countries set their own targets) replaced top-down Kyoto Protocol (1997) approach. The US withdrew under Trump (2017-2021), rejoined under Biden (Feb 2021). Current NDCs collectively put world on ~2.7 degC track — falling short of goal. Five-year ratchet mechanism requires increasing ambition. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.10) require students to evaluate Paris vs Kyoto.

The Montreal Protocol (signed 1987, entered force 1989) bans ozone-depleting chemicals (CFCs, halons, HCFCs, methyl bromide). The most successful environmental treaty in history — universally ratified by 198 parties. Triggered by 1985 discovery of the Antarctic ozone hole (Joe Farman et al.) and the 1995 Nobel Prize work of Molina, Rowland, and Crutzen establishing CFC-O3 destruction mechanism. CFC atmospheric concentrations are falling; ozone layer projected to fully recover by 2070. The 2016 Kigali Amendment phases out HFCs (CFC replacements that turned out to be potent greenhouse gases, ~1000-12000x more potent than CO2). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 7.7, 9.10) integrate the science and policy success story.

Renewable energy tax credits subsidize clean-energy deployment by reducing tax liability. Major US programs: Production Tax Credit (PTC, 1992) pays per-kWh produced by wind/biomass; Investment Tax Credit (ITC, 2005) credits 30% of solar installation cost; Inflation Reduction Act (IRA, August 2022) extended both for 10+ years and added direct-pay options, EV credits ($7,500), heat pump credits, and a $369 billion clean-energy package. China subsidizes renewables more heavily than any nation, achieving lowest global solar/wind costs. Critics argue subsidies pick winners; supporters note fossil fuels receive ~$11 million/minute globally (IMF 2023, including externalities). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 6.11) require policy-effectiveness analysis.

Net zero (carbon neutral) means anthropogenic GHG emissions are balanced by removals (carbon sinks: forests, soils, ocean uptake, direct-air-capture, BECCS — bioenergy with carbon capture). Paris Agreement (2015) requires global net zero by ~2050 to meet 1.5 degC target. Legally binding national commitments: UK (2050 — first major economy, 2019 law), Germany (2045), Sweden (2045), Japan (2050), Canada (2050), EU (2050 — Green Deal), US (2050 — Biden executive order, IRA $369B). China targets 'carbon neutrality before 2060' and emissions peak by 2030. Corporate net-zero pledges (Microsoft, Google, Amazon) often rely on carbon offsets of variable quality. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.10) require students to evaluate net-zero pathways.

Carbon monoxide (CO) is an odorless, colorless gas formed by incomplete combustion of carbon fuels (vehicle exhaust, faulty furnaces, charcoal grills, cigarettes). Binds hemoglobin 240x more tightly than O2, forming carboxyhemoglobin and causing tissue hypoxia. ~430 deaths/year in US from accidental CO poisoning (CDC). One of six EPA Criteria Pollutants under the Clean Air Act (1970, amended 1977 and 1990). NAAQS limits: 9 ppm 8-hour average. Catalytic converters (required on US vehicles since 1975 under CAA) catalyze CO + O2 yields CO2, reducing CO emissions ~90%. Urban CO has declined ~70% since 1980 (EPA). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 7.1, 7.5) test sources, health effects, and CAA controls.

Sulfur dioxide (SO2) is a colorless, pungent gas released from burning sulfur-containing fossil fuels (especially coal — up to 5% S in lignite, 0.5-3% in bituminous) and from metal smelting. In the atmosphere, SO2 + O2 + H2O yields H2SO4 (sulfuric acid), producing acid rain. The 1990 Clean Air Act Amendments (under President George H.W. Bush) created the SO2 cap-and-trade program (Title IV) that reduced US SO2 emissions ~92% by 2020 — among the most successful environmental policies in history. One of six EPA Criteria Pollutants. The 1952 London 'Great Smog' (caused by coal-soot + SO2 fog) killed 4,000-12,000 and prompted UK's 1956 Clean Air Act. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 7.1, 7.5) integrate sources and policy.

Nitrogen oxides (NOx = NO + NO2) form at high combustion temperatures when atmospheric N2 oxidizes (N2 + O2 yields 2 NO; NO + O2 yields NO2). Sources: vehicles, power plants, industrial boilers. NOx contributes to: (1) ground-level ozone smog (NOx + VOCs + sunlight yields O3 — Arie Haagen-Smit identified the LA smog mechanism 1952), (2) acid rain (NOx + H2O yields HNO3), (3) particulate matter formation (ammonium nitrate aerosols), (4) tropospheric ozone destruction of crops (~$1 billion/yr US agricultural damage). One of six EPA Criteria Pollutants. Catalytic converters reduce NOx ~90% via 2 NO + 2 CO yields N2 + 2 CO2. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 7.1, 7.3) integrate chemistry and controls.

PM2.5 is particulate matter <=2.5 micrometers diameter (~1/30 width of a human hair) — small enough to penetrate alveoli and enter the bloodstream, causing cardiovascular disease, lung cancer, asthma, and premature death. Sources: combustion (vehicles, power plants, wildfires, wood stoves), secondary formation from SO2/NOx/NH3 reacting in atmosphere. PM2.5 is responsible for ~4.2 million premature deaths/year globally (WHO 2019) — making it the largest environmental cause of death. EPA NAAQS lowered annual standard from 12 to 9 micrograms/m^3 in 2024. One of six EPA Criteria Pollutants. Wildfire smoke (intensified by climate change) now drives most worst-air-quality days in western US. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 7.1, 7.5) test sources/health.

questions.prompt: Ground-level ozone formed by NOx and VOCs.
questions.explanation: Photochemical smog (summer/'Los Angeles' smog) forms when NOx (from vehicle exhaust) reacts with VOCs (gasoline vapors, industrial solvents, biogenic terpenes) in sunlight, producing tropospheric ozone (O3) and peroxyacetyl nitrate (PAN). Mechanism identified by Caltech chemist Arie Haagen-Smit (1952). O3 at ground level is a respiratory irritant — damages lung tissue, reduces crop yields ~10-15% globally. Peaks afternoon, summer, urban areas. EPA Criteria Pollutant — NAAQS 0.070 ppm 8-hour average. Mexico City, Beijing, and Los Angeles historically had worst smog; Clean Air Act emissions controls dramatically improved LA air despite tripled population. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 7.4) require students to distinguish photochemical (NOx + VOCs + sun) from industrial (SO2-based) smog.

Secondhand smoke (environmental tobacco smoke, ETS) is the involuntary inhalation of tobacco smoke from others' cigarettes — a mixture of sidestream smoke (from burning tip) and exhaled mainstream smoke. Contains ~7,000 chemicals, 70+ carcinogens. EPA designated ETS as a Group A carcinogen (known human carcinogen) in 1992. Causes ~41,000 US deaths/yr from cardiovascular disease, lung cancer, and SIDS in infants (CDC). The 1964 US Surgeon General's report 'Smoking and Health' (Luther Terry) launched anti-tobacco policy; ETS-specific smoke-free workplace laws spread in the 1990s-2000s. ~85% of US states have smoke-free workplace laws. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 7.8) integrate health science and policy.

Radon-222 (half-life 3.8 days) is a radioactive noble gas formed by uranium-238 decay in soil and rock — seeps into basements through foundation cracks. Inhaled radon decays into alpha-emitting daughters (Po-218, Po-214) that lodge in lung tissue, causing ~21,000 US lung-cancer deaths/yr (CDC) — second leading cause of lung cancer after smoking. EPA action level: 4 pCi/L (148 Bq/m^3). The 1988 Indoor Radon Abatement Act mandates EPA testing programs. Mitigation: sub-slab depressurization (pipe vents radon from beneath foundation) typically costs $800-2,500. Geographic risk: highest in PA, NJ, OH (uranium-rich shale and bedrock). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 7.8) test indoor-air hazards and mitigation.

Asbestos is a group of six naturally occurring silicate minerals (chrysotile most common) prized for heat resistance and tensile strength — widely used in insulation, brake pads, fireproofing, and floor tiles before 1980. Inhaled fibers cause asbestosis (lung scarring), lung cancer, and mesothelioma (rare aggressive cancer of pleura). Latency 20-50 years; ~107,000 global deaths/yr (WHO). EPA's 1989 Asbestos Ban and Phase-Out Rule was largely overturned by court in 1991 — US never fully banned asbestos until 2024 EPA chrysotile ban. ~55 countries have full bans (UK 1999, Australia 2003, EU 2005). Schools must follow AHERA (Asbestos Hazard Emergency Response Act 1986). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 7.8) integrate health/policy.

Formaldehyde (HCHO) is a colorless, pungent VOC used in particleboard, plywood, foam insulation, fabrics, and embalming fluid. Off-gases from new furniture, carpets, and building materials — concentrations in new homes can exceed 0.1 ppm. Causes eye/respiratory irritation; classified as Group 1 human carcinogen by IARC in 2006 (linked to nasopharyngeal cancer, leukemia). Sick Building Syndrome often involves formaldehyde plus VOCs. The Formaldehyde Standards for Composite Wood Products Act (2010) set EPA emission limits for pressed-wood products effective 2018. The Hurricane Katrina FEMA trailers (2005) had elevated formaldehyde — public-health scandal. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 7.8) integrate VOC chemistry, health, and regulation.

Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs) are carbon-containing chemicals that vaporize at room temperature — emitted from paints, solvents, cleaning products, adhesives, gasoline, and biogenic sources (forests emit terpenes). VOCs contribute to: (1) photochemical smog formation (VOCs + NOx + sunlight yields O3 — Arie Haagen-Smit 1952 Caltech), (2) indoor air pollution causing Sick Building Syndrome, (3) some are direct carcinogens (benzene, formaldehyde). EPA regulates VOC content of consumer products under Clean Air Act. Low-VOC paints (post-1990s reformulation) reduce emissions ~90%. ASHRAE Standard 62 governs building ventilation rates. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 7.4, 7.8) integrate indoor/outdoor effects and source controls.

Acid rain is precipitation with pH below 5.6 (natural CO2-saturated rainwater's equilibrium pH). Forms when SO2 + H2O + O2 yields H2SO4 (sulfuric acid) and NOx + H2O yields HNO3 (nitric acid). Acidity scale is logarithmic — pH 4 rain is 10x more acidic than pH 5. Worst-affected pH measurements: 2.4 in Wheeling West Virginia (1979); 1.5 in fog at Mount Mitchell NC. Damages forests (Adirondack red spruce, Black Forest in Germany), aquatic ecosystems (Adirondack lakes fish kills below pH 5), buildings (marble dissolution — US Capitol, Greek Parthenon), and statues. Robert Angus Smith coined 'acid rain' in 1872 studying Manchester pollution. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 7.5) integrate chemistry, ecology, and policy.

SO2 emissions from coal combustion (high-sulfur Appalachian and Midwest coal) are the largest single cause of acid rain. SO2 + O2 + H2O yields H2SO4 (sulfuric acid). The 1990 Clean Air Act Amendments (under President George H.W. Bush, signed Nov 15 1990) established the SO2 cap-and-trade Acid Rain Program (Title IV): EPA capped total US power-plant SO2, distributed tradable allowances, and let market find lowest-cost reductions. US SO2 dropped ~92% by 2020 (from ~16 million tons in 1990 to <1.5 million tons), benefit-cost ratio ~25-40:1 ($122 billion/yr benefits, $3 billion/yr costs). One of the most successful environmental policies in history. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 7.5) require integration with market-based regulation.

questions.prompt: Damage to forests from acid rain.
questions.explanation: Acid rain damages forests primarily through soil acidification — leaching essential base cations (Ca^2+, Mg^2+, K+) from soil while mobilizing toxic aluminum (Al^3+) that damages tree roots. Adirondack red spruce, sugar maple in Vermont, and Germany's Black Forest (Waldsterben — 'forest death,' peaking 1980s) all showed acid-rain damage. Foliage damage also direct — acid mist burns leaves, especially at high elevations. The Hubbard Brook Experimental Forest (NH) long-term study (Gene Likens et al., since 1963) provided the foundational science linking acid rain to forest decline; Likens shared the 2001 Tyler Prize. Recovery slow even after SO2 reductions — depleted soil cations take decades to replenish via weathering. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 7.5) test forest-soil acidification chemistry.

questions.prompt: Damage to lakes from acid rain.
questions.explanation: Acid rain acidifies lakes and streams, killing fish and amphibians. Below pH 5, fish eggs fail to hatch; below pH 4.5, adult fish (especially salmon, trout) die. Aluminum mobilized from acidified soils enters streams and clogs fish gills. Adirondack Mountains (NY): >300 high-elevation lakes were fishless by 1980s due to acid rain. Scandinavian lakes lost salmon. The 1980 NAPAP (National Acid Precipitation Assessment Program) Act funded a $500 million 10-year study confirming damage and informing the 1990 Clean Air Act SO2 controls. Some lakes are slowly recovering 30+ years after SO2 reductions (~92% decline). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 7.5, 8.2) integrate aquatic ecology, geochemistry, and policy effectiveness.

questions.prompt: Law reducing acid rain in US.
questions.explanation: The Clean Air Act (CAA), signed by President Nixon December 31, 1970, gave EPA (also 1970) authority to set National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS) for six Criteria Pollutants (PM, O3, CO, SO2, NOx, Pb) and emission standards for stationary and mobile sources. The 1977 Amendments added PSD and NSR provisions. The 1990 Amendments (George H.W. Bush) added Title IV SO2 cap-and-trade for acid rain control (reduced SO2 ~92% by 2020), Title VI ozone-depleting chemical phase-out, and Title III hazardous air pollutants (HAPs). EPA cost-benefit analysis (1999): CAA saved 230,000 lives/yr by 2020 with ~30:1 benefit-cost ratio. The 2007 Massachusetts v. EPA Supreme Court ruling included CO2 under CAA. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 7.5).

The ozone layer is a region of the stratosphere (15-35 km altitude) where O3 concentrations peak at ~10 ppm — enough to absorb 97-99% of incoming UV-B and all UV-C radiation, shielding life from DNA damage, skin cancer, cataracts, and ecosystem damage. Stratospheric ozone formation: O2 + UV yields 2 O, then O + O2 yields O3 (Sydney Chapman 1930). Without the ozone layer, the evolution of land life (~400 Ma ago) would have been impossible. The Antarctic ozone hole was discovered by Joe Farman, Brian Gardiner, and Jonathan Shanklin (Nature 1985) using Halley Bay BAS data — losses started 1970s, peaked 1990s-2000s, slowly recovering since Montreal Protocol. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 7.7) test the photochemistry.

Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs, marketed as Freon by DuPont) were developed by Thomas Midgley Jr. in 1928 as non-toxic, non-flammable refrigerants and aerosol propellants. CFC-11 and CFC-12 are most common. In 1974, Mario Molina and F. Sherwood Rowland (Nature paper) predicted CFCs would migrate to stratosphere over decades, photodissociate, and release Cl atoms that catalytically destroy O3 — one Cl atom can destroy ~100,000 O3 molecules. The Antarctic ozone hole discovery (Farman 1985) confirmed predictions. Molina, Rowland, and Paul Crutzen shared the 1995 Nobel Prize in Chemistry. The Montreal Protocol (1987) banned CFCs globally. CFC atmospheric concentrations peaked ~1994 and are now declining; the ozone layer is projected to fully recover by 2070. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 7.7) integrate.

The Antarctic ozone hole is the seasonal severe depletion of stratospheric O3 over Antarctica each Southern Hemisphere spring (Sep-Nov). Mechanism (Susan Solomon et al. 1986): polar stratospheric clouds (PSCs, form at -78 degC) provide surfaces for chlorine-activation reactions; when sunlight returns in spring, activated Cl rapidly destroys O3. Discovered by Joe Farman, Brian Gardiner, and Jonathan Shanklin (British Antarctic Survey, Nature May 1985) from Halley Bay measurements. Hole peaked ~27 million km^2 in 2006 (larger than North America). Montreal Protocol (1987) banned CFCs; CFCs peaked atmospherically ~1994; ozone hole began healing ~2000; full recovery projected by 2070. WMO/UNEP 2022 assessment confirms recovery is on track. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 7.7) integrate.

The Montreal Protocol on Substances that Deplete the Ozone Layer, signed September 16, 1987 in Montreal, entered force January 1, 1989. Phases out CFCs, halons, methyl bromide, HCFCs, and other ozone-depleting substances. 198 parties (universal ratification — only environmental treaty with this status). Strengthened by London (1990), Copenhagen (1992), and Beijing (1999) amendments accelerating phase-out timelines. Kofi Annan called it 'perhaps the single most successful international agreement to date.' Triggered by 1985 Antarctic ozone-hole discovery and 1995 Nobel Prize (Molina, Rowland, Crutzen). Co-benefit: phasing out CFCs (also potent GHGs) avoided ~135 GtCO2-equivalent emissions through 2010 — larger than Kyoto Protocol reductions. The 2016 Kigali Amendment phases out HFCs. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 7.7, 9.10) integrate.

UV-B radiation damages DNA in skin cells, causing skin cancer — basal cell carcinoma (most common, rarely fatal), squamous cell carcinoma, and malignant melanoma (~100,000 US cases/yr, 8,000 deaths/yr). Each 1% loss of stratospheric ozone increases UV-B at surface ~2%, increasing skin-cancer incidence ~3-4%. Australia (high UV, fair-skinned population) has highest skin-cancer rates globally (~2,000 melanoma deaths/yr per 25 million people); Slip-Slop-Slap campaign (since 1981) and SunSmart programs cut melanoma deaths ~50%. UV damage also causes cataracts (3 million cases/yr globally per WHO). Sunscreens (SPF 30+) and shade reduce risk. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 7.7) connect stratospheric ozone depletion to public-health outcomes and ozone-layer recovery benefits.

Water vapor (H2O) is the most abundant greenhouse gas, responsible for ~50-60% of natural greenhouse warming. Unlike CO2 and CH4, water vapor is in rapid equilibrium with surface water (residence time ~9 days) and acts as a positive feedback to CO2-driven warming — warmer air holds more H2O (Clausius-Clapeyron: ~7% per degC), amplifying initial warming by ~2x. Direct human emissions of water vapor (from combustion, irrigation) have negligible long-term effect because the atmosphere returns to equilibrium quickly. Stratospheric water vapor (from CH4 oxidation) has been increasing and contributes ~1/3 of greenhouse forcing growth (Solomon et al. 2010). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.3) require students to distinguish forcing from feedback.

CO2 is the dominant human-caused greenhouse gas — responsible for ~76% of anthropogenic GHG warming (IPCC AR6 2021). Atmospheric concentration has risen from pre-industrial ~280 ppm to ~420 ppm by 2024 — 50% increase, highest in 3+ million years per Antarctic ice core data (EPICA Dome C 800,000-year record, Luthi et al. 2008 Nature). Charles David Keeling began continuous measurements at Mauna Loa, Hawaii in 1958 (the Keeling Curve). Atmospheric lifetime ~300-1,000 years (some persists 100,000+ years), so CO2 accumulates. Sources: fossil-fuel combustion (~75% of emissions), deforestation (~10%), cement production (~5%). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.3) require integration with mitigation policy.

Methane (CH4) is the second-most-important anthropogenic GHG (~16% of warming). Global Warming Potential (GWP): ~80x CO2 over 20 years, ~27x over 100 years (IPCC AR6). Atmospheric lifetime ~12 years (much shorter than CO2's centuries — methane reductions yield rapid climate benefits). Concentration rose from pre-industrial ~722 ppb to ~1923 ppb in 2023 (NOAA). Sources: oil and gas extraction (fugitive emissions, ~25%), livestock enteric fermentation (~25%), rice paddies (~10%), landfills (~15%), wetlands (natural ~30%), permafrost thaw (rising). Global Methane Pledge (COP26 2021): 100+ countries pledged to cut methane 30% by 2030. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.3) require GWP calculations and policy analysis.

Nitrous oxide (N2O) is the third-most-important anthropogenic GHG (~6% of warming). GWP: 273x CO2 (IPCC AR6, 100-yr) and atmospheric lifetime ~114 years. Concentration rose from pre-industrial ~270 ppb to ~336 ppb by 2023 (NOAA). Sources: agricultural soils (~75%, especially from synthetic N fertilizer denitrification), livestock manure, fossil-fuel combustion, industrial processes (nitric acid production), biomass burning. Also depletes stratospheric ozone (now the most significant ozone-depleting substance emitted today, since CFCs have been phased out). Mitigation: precision N fertilizer application, nitrification inhibitors, cover crops. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.3) require students to connect agriculture to climate forcing.

Hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs) replaced CFCs and HCFCs as refrigerants and foam-blowing agents under the Montreal Protocol (1987). HFCs don't deplete ozone (no chlorine) but are potent greenhouse gases — GWPs range 1,000-12,000 over 100 years. HFC-23 has GWP ~14,700 (highest). Concentration rising ~10%/year — fastest of any GHG. The 2016 Kigali Amendment to the Montreal Protocol (entered force Jan 2019) phases down HFCs globally — projected to avoid 0.5 degC warming by 2100, one of the largest single climate-policy wins. US AIM Act (2020) codifies Kigali domestically. Alternatives: HFOs (hydrofluoroolefins, low-GWP), CO2, ammonia, and propane refrigerants. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 7.7, 9.3, 9.10) integrate ozone and climate policy.

Water pollution is the contamination of water bodies (rivers, lakes, groundwater, oceans) by chemicals, pathogens, sediments, heat, or radioactive materials beyond natural background. Modern US regulation began after the Cuyahoga River in Cleveland caught fire on June 22, 1969 (the river had caught fire 13 times since 1868), and Time magazine's photos catalyzed the environmental movement. Congress passed the Clean Water Act (Federal Water Pollution Control Act Amendments) on October 18, 1972, establishing EPA authority to regulate point-source discharges via NPDES permits. ~40% of US rivers and lakes remain impaired (EPA 2022). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 8.1) integrate sources, types, and Clean Water Act provisions.

Point-source pollution enters water from a single identifiable discharge point — pipes, ditches, tunnels, wells, factory outfalls, sewage treatment plant outfalls, oil tanker spills. Regulated under the Clean Water Act 1972 via NPDES (National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System) permits — each facility must obtain a permit limiting discharge concentrations and load. Point sources have been dramatically controlled since CWA — Cuyahoga River (Cleveland 1969 fire), Hudson River (GE PCB contamination, 1947-1977), and others have substantially improved. Compare with nonpoint sources (runoff, more difficult to regulate). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 8.1, 8.2) require students to identify point sources, predict regulation, and contrast with nonpoint.

Nonpoint-source pollution comes from diffuse, hard-to-track sources: agricultural runoff (N, P fertilizer; pesticides; sediment), urban runoff (oils, road salt, pet waste, particulates from brake wear), atmospheric deposition (mercury from coal-fired power plants), and forestry. Comprises the largest unregulated water-pollution problem in the US — the Clean Water Act (1972) was designed for point sources via NPDES permits, leaving nonpoint sources largely unaddressed except via state water-quality planning under Section 319. Best Management Practices (BMPs): cover crops, conservation tillage, riparian buffers, terracing, no-till farming. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 8.1, 8.2) require integrated control proposals.

Eutrophication is the over-enrichment of water with nutrients (N, P) causing explosive algal/cyanobacterial growth. Natural eutrophication takes millennia; cultural eutrophication from agriculture/sewage/detergents (banned in US laundry 1994) accelerates it to years. Process: nutrient loading yields algal bloom; algae die; bacterial decomposition consumes dissolved oxygen (BOD spike); creating hypoxic dead zone (DO<2 mg/L) that suffocates fish. The Gulf of Mexico dead zone (Mississippi River agricultural runoff) reaches ~6,000-8,000 mi^2 annually. Lake Erie 2014 algal bloom shut down Toledo's drinking water (cyanobacteria release microcystin toxin). Eutrophication is the most widespread water-quality problem globally. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 8.4, 8.5) require integration.

Dissolved oxygen (DO) is O2 dissolved in water, essential for aerobic aquatic life. Cold water holds more DO than warm (Henry's law); fast-flowing/turbulent water more than stagnant. Healthy DO >6 mg/L for trout, >2 mg/L for most fish; hypoxia at <2 mg/L; anoxia at <0.5 mg/L. Eutrophication-driven dead zones: when algae die and bacteria decompose them, BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand) spikes and consumes DO, suffocating fish. Gulf of Mexico dead zone (~6,000 mi^2 average, ~22,000 mi^2 peak 2017); Chesapeake Bay; Baltic Sea (largest in the world). Climate change reduces DO via warming (lower solubility) and stratification. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 8.5, 8.6) require students to predict DO impacts and integrate eutrophication-dead-zone chemistry.

Primary sewage treatment is mechanical/physical: screening removes large debris (sticks, rags, plastic); grit chambers settle sand/gravel; primary clarifiers (sedimentation tanks) settle suspended solids and skim grease/oil. Removes ~50-70% of suspended solids and ~25-40% of BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand). Resulting sludge is sent for further treatment or disposal. Primary treatment alone is insufficient — secondary biological treatment is required by the Clean Water Act (1972) for US municipalities. Before secondary treatment was required, many cities discharged primary-treated sewage directly into rivers (Boston Harbor until 1991 court order, $4.5 billion Deer Island upgrade). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 8.13) test the three-stage sequence.

Secondary sewage treatment uses aerobic bacteria to biologically decompose organic matter. Two main systems: activated sludge (aeration tanks bubble air through wastewater + bacterial colonies; bacteria consume dissolved organics) or trickling filters (wastewater sprays over rock/plastic beds coated with biofilm). Removes ~85-90% of BOD and suspended solids. Required for all US municipal wastewater under the Clean Water Act (1972 — Section 301 demanded 'secondary treatment of all publicly owned treatment works' by 1977; deadlines extended). Secondary treatment doesn't remove dissolved nutrients (N, P) — those require tertiary treatment. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 8.13) require students to identify what each stage removes and what it leaves behind.

Tertiary (advanced) sewage treatment removes specific dissolved pollutants left after secondary treatment — phosphorus (chemical precipitation with alum/iron salts), nitrogen (biological nitrification-denitrification), heavy metals, pharmaceuticals, and pathogens. Required where receiving waters are nutrient-sensitive (Chesapeake Bay watershed, Lake Tahoe). More expensive than secondary — adds ~50-100% to treatment costs. Membrane bioreactors and reverse osmosis can produce water of drinking quality (used in Singapore's NEWater, Orange County CA's Groundwater Replenishment). Removing pharmaceuticals (estrogens, antidepressants, antibiotics) is an active research area — current secondary treatment leaves ~50-90% in effluent. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 8.13) test stage sequence and removal effectiveness.

Chlorination disinfects sewage effluent and drinking water by killing pathogens with chlorine (gaseous Cl2 or sodium hypochlorite NaOCl). Began in US drinking water in Jersey City NJ (1908, John Leal MD), dramatically reducing typhoid and cholera (typhoid death rate dropped 90% in early 20th century). Disinfection byproducts (DBPs) — trihalomethanes (THMs, including chloroform) and haloacetic acids — form when chlorine reacts with organic matter; classified as suspected carcinogens, regulated under Safe Drinking Water Act (1974). Alternative disinfectants: chloramines (longer-lasting residual), ozone (no DBPs but no residual), UV light (no chemicals but no residual). The 1993 Milwaukee Cryptosporidium outbreak (400,000 sickened, 100 deaths) prompted SDWA Surface Water Treatment Rule. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 8.13) test trade-offs.

Sewage sludge (biosolids when treated to Class A standards) is the semi-solid residue from sewage-treatment processes. US generates ~7 million dry tons/year. Disposal options: land application as fertilizer (~50% — requires meeting EPA 503 standards for pathogens, heavy metals, organic contaminants), incineration (~15%), landfilling (~30%), surface impoundment. Class A biosolids (pathogen-free) can be sold to public; Class B (treated but with detectable pathogens) restricted to non-food crops. Concerns: heavy metals (Cd, Pb), pharmaceuticals, PFAS (forever chemicals) accumulating in agricultural soils. Some states (Maine 2022) banning land application due to PFAS contamination. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 8.13) integrate disposal options and emerging contaminants.

Sanitary landfills are engineered facilities for solid-waste disposal: compacted layers covered daily with soil; bottom-lined with clay + plastic (HDPE) to prevent leachate (contaminated drainage) escape; leachate collection and treatment; methane gas recovery (~20% of US municipal solid waste landfills capture methane for electricity). Regulated under RCRA Subtitle D (1976, strengthened 1991). US generates ~292 million tons MSW/yr; ~50% landfilled (down from 90% in 1960s). Concerns: leachate contamination, methane (CH4 is ~80x more potent GHG than CO2 over 20 years), wildlife (gulls), siting NIMBY conflicts. EU has largely banned organic landfilling; Japan incinerates ~75%. Fresh Kills (Staten Island, opened 1948, closed 2001) was world's largest. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 8.10) integrate.

Incineration (waste-to-energy, WTE) burns solid waste at 850-1,200 degC, reducing volume ~90% and mass ~75%; modern facilities recover heat for electricity (waste-to-energy provides ~12% of US electricity in WTE plants, vs ~22% in EU). Concerns: dioxins/furans (now controlled by modern bag filters and lime injection, US emissions down ~97% since 1990), heavy metals in fly ash (hazardous), CO2 emissions (~1 ton CO2 per ton waste). Sweden incinerates ~50% of MSW and imports waste from Norway/UK. Florida, NY, MA have largest US WTE capacity. Critics argue WTE competes with recycling. The 1990 Clean Air Act amendments tightened incinerator emissions standards. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 8.10) integrate trade-offs.

Recycling reprocesses waste materials into new products, conserving virgin resources and reducing landfill demand. US recycling rate: ~32% (EPA 2018) — among highest for cardboard (~91%) and aluminum cans (~50%); among lowest for plastic (~9%). Aluminum recycling saves 95% energy vs primary smelting; paper saves 60%, glass 40%. China's 2018 'National Sword' policy stopped accepting US recyclables, triggering US recycling crisis as much material went to landfill. The 1976 Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA) authorizes EPA's solid-waste programs. 'Wishcycling' (putting non-recyclable items in bin hoping they'll be recycled) contaminates streams. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 8.10) require students to compare reduce/reuse/recycle hierarchy.

Composting is the controlled aerobic decomposition of organic waste (food scraps, yard waste, paper) by bacteria and fungi into nutrient-rich humus. Diverts organic matter (~30% of US MSW = food + yard waste) from landfills, where it would generate methane anaerobically (~17% of US methane emissions come from landfills). Properly managed compost piles reach 55-65 degC, killing pathogens and weed seeds. Process takes 3-6 months. ~50 US cities mandate residential food-waste composting (San Francisco 2009, Seattle 2014, NYC by 2025). California SB 1383 (2022) requires 75% organics diversion by 2025. Vermiculture uses worms (Eisenia fetida) to accelerate composting. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 8.10) integrate landfill methane reduction, soil benefits, and policy.

Source reduction (waste prevention, 'precycling') eliminates waste before it's generated — the top of EPA's waste hierarchy (Reduce > Reuse > Recycle > Recover energy > Dispose). Examples: lightweighting packaging (modern plastic bottle uses ~40% less material than 1970s), bulk purchasing, durable products, paperless billing, reusable bags. Most effective and least costly approach because it eliminates collection, processing, and disposal entirely. The 2008 EU Waste Framework Directive enshrined the hierarchy. Several US cities have plastic-bag bans (CA 2014, NY 2020). Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) laws (Maine 2021 first US packaging EPR) shift waste-management costs to producers. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 8.11) require students to rank options by environmental benefit.

Hazardous waste exhibits one of four characteristics under RCRA (Resource Conservation and Recovery Act 1976): ignitability (flash point <60 degC), corrosivity (pH <2 or >12.5), reactivity (explosive, reacts violently with water), or toxicity (TCLP leach test). Listed hazardous wastes include specific industrial wastes (F, K, P, U codes). US generates ~35 million tons hazardous waste/year. Cradle-to-grave tracking via the manifest system. The Love Canal disaster (Niagara Falls NY, residential development built on top of Hooker Chemical's 22,000-ton chemical dump 1942-53; Lois Gibbs led residents 1978; President Carter declared emergency 1978, evacuating ~900 families) prompted CERCLA (Superfund) 1980. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 8.12) integrate.

The Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA), enacted October 21, 1976, governs cradle-to-grave management of solid and hazardous waste. Subtitle C covers hazardous waste (definition, generator/transporter/TSDF requirements, manifest system); Subtitle D covers non-hazardous solid waste (landfill standards); Subtitle I covers underground storage tanks. EPA implements RCRA in cooperation with state programs. Strengthened by Hazardous and Solid Waste Amendments (HSWA 1984) banning land disposal of untreated hazardous wastes. Compare with CERCLA (Superfund 1980) which addresses HISTORIC abandoned sites; RCRA prevents NEW contamination. The 1976 Toxic Substances Control Act (TSCA) regulates chemicals in commerce. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 8.12) integrate.

The Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation, and Liability Act (CERCLA), signed by President Carter on December 11, 1980, created the Superfund to clean up abandoned hazardous-waste sites. Triggered by the Love Canal disaster (Niagara Falls NY, 1978 emergency declaration) and the Valley of the Drums (KY). Establishes joint-and-several liability for Potentially Responsible Parties (PRPs) — original polluter, current owner, even lender. National Priorities List (NPL) ranks worst sites — currently ~1,330. Worst US sites: Hanford WA (nuclear weapons), Hudson River (GE PCBs), Berkeley Pit Montana (copper mining). Cleanups cost average $50 million/site; ~$30 billion spent total. Strengthened by SARA Amendments (1986) and Brownfields Act (2002). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 8.12) integrate.

Bioaccumulation is the buildup of persistent, fat-soluble (lipophilic) toxins in individual organisms over time, as intake (food, water) exceeds elimination. Often paired with biomagnification (concentration increasing up trophic levels). DDT (dichlorodiphenyl-trichloroethane, synthesized 1874, insecticidal use Paul Muller Nobel 1948) bioaccumulates in fat tissue and biomagnifies in food webs — Rachel Carson's 'Silent Spring' (1962) documented bird-population crashes from DDT eggshell-thinning. By 1972, bald eagles, brown pelicans, and ospreys were near extinction. EPA banned DDT in 1972 (William Ruckelshaus); populations recovered. Other bioaccumulating toxins: PCBs, mercury, dioxins, PFAS. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 8.7) integrate.

Biomagnification (food-chain magnification) is the increasing concentration of persistent toxins at each ascending trophic level. Mechanism: organisms eat many lower-level prey, transferring their accumulated toxin load; the toxin is fat-soluble and slowly excreted; each trophic transfer roughly 10x the concentration. Classic DDT example: water 0.000003 ppm, zooplankton 0.04 ppm, small fish 0.5 ppm, large fish 2 ppm, fish-eating birds 25 ppm (Woodwell et al. 1967 in 'Persistent Pesticides in the Marine Environment'). Top predators (bald eagles, ospreys, brown pelicans) suffered eggshell thinning from DDT-DDE, near-extinction by 1970, recovery after EPA's 1972 ban. Mercury in tuna/swordfish (FDA advisories for pregnant women), PCBs in lake trout. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 8.7).

LD50 (median lethal dose) is the dose of a substance that kills 50% of a test population in standardized animal trials — the most common quantitative toxicity measure. J.W. Trevan introduced it in 1927. Lower LD50 = more toxic. Examples (mg/kg body weight in rats): caffeine ~192, aspirin ~200, table salt ~3,000, vitamin C ~11,900, botulinum toxin ~0.000001 (most toxic known), TCDD dioxin ~0.02. LD50 from animals is extrapolated to humans with safety factors (usually 100x or 1000x) to set Reference Doses (RfDs). The 1976 Toxic Substances Control Act (TSCA) requires LD50 testing for new chemicals. Limitations: ignores chronic low-dose effects, individual variation, developmental toxicity. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 8.8) test dose-response interpretation.

Dose-response curves plot biological response vs dose, showing how toxicity scales with exposure. Most curves are sigmoidal (S-shaped): low doses produce no observable effect (NOAEL, no observed adverse effect level), then response rises through LD50 to maximum effect. Threshold dose-response (most chemicals) assumes some safe dose below which no effect; non-threshold (carcinogens, mutagens, ionizing radiation) assumes any dose carries some risk. Paracelsus (16th century) coined 'the dose makes the poison.' Some curves are non-monotonic (e.g., endocrine disruptors) where low doses cause MORE effect than higher (hormesis). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 8.8) require students to read curves, identify NOAEL, LD50, and apply safety factors.

Carcinogens are substances that cause cancer by damaging DNA or disrupting cell-cycle control. International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC, WHO) classifies: Group 1 (carcinogenic to humans — tobacco smoke, asbestos, benzene, formaldehyde, X-rays, processed meat, alcohol, sunlight UV); Group 2A (probably carcinogenic — glyphosate, red meat, hot beverages >65 degC); Group 2B (possibly carcinogenic — DDT, lead, coffee removed 2016); Group 3 (not classifiable). Cancer risk is typically modeled with non-threshold linear dose-response (any dose carries some probability). The 1976 Toxic Substances Control Act (TSCA) and 1958 Delaney Clause (Food Additives Amendment) restricted carcinogens in food. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 8.8) integrate IARC, dose-response, and regulation.

Teratogens cause structural birth defects when fetus is exposed in utero. Classic examples: thalidomide (sedative marketed 1957-1962, caused ~10,000 cases of phocomelia — limb deformities; Frances Kelsey at FDA blocked US approval, won Distinguished Federal Civilian Service award 1962); diethylstilbestrol (DES, prescribed for miscarriage prevention 1940-71, caused vaginal cancer in daughters); rubella virus; alcohol (fetal alcohol syndrome); thalidomide tragedy prompted 1962 Kefauver-Harris Amendment requiring safety AND efficacy testing for drugs. Methylmercury (Minamata disease, Japan 1956 — Chisso Corporation discharged methylmercury into Minamata Bay; ~2,000 deaths, congenital deformities). Lead, smoking, certain antibiotics. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 8.9) require students to identify teratogens and connect to regulation.

Endocrine disruptors mimic, block, or interfere with hormones at low (often sub-toxic) doses. The hypothesis was developed by Theo Colborn et al. in 'Our Stolen Future' (1996). Examples: DDT (banned 1972), PCBs (banned 1979), DES (banned 1971), BPA (in polycarbonate plastics, food can linings — feminizes male fish), phthalates (plasticizers, alter male reproductive development), atrazine (herbicide — Tyrone Hayes's 2002 PNAS paper showed feminization of male frogs at 0.1 ppb), PFAS ('forever chemicals' in non-stick coatings, firefighting foam). Endocrine-disrupting effects often follow non-monotonic dose-response, complicating regulation. EU REACH Authorization tightens restrictions. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 8.9) require students to integrate dose-response, regulation, and reproductive ecology.

Climate change refers to long-term shifts in Earth's temperature, precipitation, and weather patterns — driven since ~1850 primarily by anthropogenic GHG emissions. Global mean temperature has risen ~1.1 degC above pre-industrial (1850-1900 baseline) per IPCC AR6 (2021), the fastest rate in at least 2,000 years. Svante Arrhenius (1896 paper 'On the Influence of Carbonic Acid in the Air upon the Temperature of the Ground') first calculated that doubling CO2 would warm Earth ~5 degC. Charles David Keeling began continuous CO2 measurements at Mauna Loa Hawaii in 1958 — the Keeling Curve. Current CO2 ~420 ppm, highest in 3+ million years. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.4) integrate atmospheric physics with policy.

CO2 is the dominant anthropogenic GHG — responsible for ~76% of human-caused warming (IPCC AR6 2021). Atmospheric concentration has risen from pre-industrial ~280 ppm (Antarctic ice cores: EPICA Dome C 800,000-year record, Luthi et al. 2008 Nature) to ~420 ppm by 2024 — 50% increase, highest in 3+ million years. Charles David Keeling started continuous Mauna Loa measurements 1958 — the iconic Keeling Curve. Long atmospheric lifetime (~300-1,000 years; some persists 100,000+ years) means emissions accumulate. Sources: fossil-fuel combustion (~75% of emissions), deforestation (~10%), cement production (~5%). Carbon budgets: ~400 GtCO2 remaining for 50% chance of 1.5 degC — emitting current 40 GtCO2/yr exhausts in ~10 years. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.4).

Thermal expansion contributes ~50% of observed sea-level rise (about 1.4 mm/yr of total ~3.4 mm/yr). As ocean water warms, it expands (negative thermal expansion coefficient near 4 degC reverses — warm water occupies more volume per gram). The other ~50% of sea-level rise is from melting land ice: Greenland ice sheet (~280 Gt/yr, accelerating), Antarctica (~150 Gt/yr, accelerating), and mountain glaciers (~280 Gt/yr). Total global mean sea level has risen ~21-24 cm since 1880 (NOAA tide gauges + satellite altimetry). IPCC AR6 projects 0.3-1.0 m by 2100 under intermediate scenarios. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.5) require students to partition sea-level-rise contributions and predict coastal impacts.

Coastal flooding from sea-level rise threatens ~600 million people globally living below 10 m elevation. ~13 million Americans in 100-year floodplains; Miami-Dade FL faces ~3 ft rise by 2100 (NOAA Intermediate-High scenario). Storm surge atop higher baseline causes catastrophic flooding — Hurricane Sandy 2012 caused $70 billion damages in NY/NJ; Hurricane Harvey 2017 in Houston caused $125 billion (NWS). 'Sunny day flooding' (high-tide flooding) frequency in US has increased ~5x since 1960. The 2005 Hurricane Katrina disaster (1,800 deaths, $125 billion) showed compounding climate vulnerability. Mangroves and salt marshes (which dampen storm surge ~50%) lost to development worsen impacts. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.5) require integrated coastal-impact analysis.

The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) was established 1988 by UNEP and WMO to provide scientific assessments of climate change for governments. Comprises ~200 scientists who synthesize peer-reviewed literature. Six Assessment Reports (AR) issued 1990, 1995, 2001, 2007, 2014, 2021-23. AR6 (2021-23) concluded: 'unequivocal that human influence has warmed the atmosphere, ocean and land'; warming will reach 1.5 degC by ~2030-2035; limiting to 1.5 degC requires global emissions peak by 2025 and halve by 2030. Shared 2007 Nobel Peace Prize with Al Gore for 'their efforts to build up and disseminate greater knowledge about man-made climate change.' Triggered Kyoto Protocol (1997) and Paris Agreement (2015). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.4, 9.10) integrate science-policy.

questions.prompt: CO2 absorbing into oceans causes this.
questions.explanation: Ocean acidification is the decline of seawater pH due to absorption of atmospheric CO2: CO2 + H2O yields H2CO3 (carbonic acid), which dissociates to H+ + HCO3-. Oceans have absorbed ~30% of anthropogenic CO2, reducing surface pH from ~8.2 to ~8.1 since 1850 — a 26% increase in [H+] (logarithmic scale). IPCC projects pH ~7.8 by 2100 under high-emissions scenarios (RCP 8.5). 'The other CO2 problem' (companion to atmospheric warming). Threatens calcifying organisms (corals, pteropods, oysters, mussels) — saturation state of aragonite drops, making shell-building difficult or impossible. Sometimes called the 'evil twin' of climate change. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.6) require students to write the chemistry and predict ecological impacts.

Coral reefs are most threatened by ocean acidification AND ocean warming (a double assault). Acidification reduces aragonite saturation, making CaCO3 skeletal growth harder; warming causes coral bleaching (expulsion of symbiotic Symbiodinium algae) when seawater exceeds species-specific threshold (~1-2 degC above summer average). The Great Barrier Reef (Australia, 1,400 miles, 2,900 individual reefs) has experienced six mass-bleaching events: 1998, 2002, 2016, 2017, 2020, 2022; the 2016-17 events killed 50% of shallow corals. Coral reefs support ~25% of marine species, provide ~$30 billion/yr in ecosystem services (fisheries, tourism, coastal protection), and protect coastlines from storm surge. IPCC AR6: at 1.5 degC, 70-90% of coral reefs lost; at 2 degC, >99% lost. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.6).

Coral bleaching is the stress response where coral polyps expel their symbiotic Symbiodinium dinoflagellate algae (zooxanthellae), losing color and ~90% of their energy supply. Triggered by elevated sea-surface temperatures (1-2 degC above summer average for 4+ weeks), pollution, sedimentation, or freshwater influx. If conditions return to normal within weeks, corals can recover; if prolonged, corals starve and die. Bleached reefs are skeletal-white. Mass bleaching events: 1998 (global, killed ~16% of world's corals), 2010, 2014-17 (longest event recorded), 2020, 2022. NOAA Coral Reef Watch tracks heat stress (Degree Heating Weeks). IPCC AR6: 1.5 degC warming = 70-90% reef loss; 2 degC = >99% loss. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.6) integrate physiology with climate scenarios.

Ocean acidification reduces seawater [CO3^2-] (carbonate ions) and lowers the saturation state of CaCO3 minerals (aragonite, calcite), making it harder for organisms to build shells and skeletons — and dissolving existing shells in undersaturated waters. Polar oceans are first affected (cold water holds more CO2; aragonite saturation Omega < 1 already in parts of Arctic). Pteropods ('sea butterflies' — pelagic snails) in the Southern Ocean show shell dissolution (Bednarsek et al. 2012 documented thinning, pitting). Oyster larvae (Crassostrea gigas) in Pacific Northwest hatcheries failed in 2007-08 when upwelling brought acidified water — Whiskey Creek hatchery (Netarts OR) traced cause to OA. Pacific NW shellfish industry implements pH buffering. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.6) require students to write CaCO3 dissolution chemistry.

questions.prompt: pH change in ocean since pre-industrial.
questions.explanation: Ocean surface pH has dropped from pre-industrial ~8.2 to ~8.1 — a 0.1 pH unit decrease. Because pH is logarithmic, this represents a ~26-30% increase in hydrogen-ion concentration [H+]. By 2100 under high-emissions scenarios (IPCC AR6 SSP5-8.5), pH is projected to fall to ~7.8 (~150% more acidic than pre-industrial). The rate of acidification is ~10-100x faster than any natural change in the past 55 million years (Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum, 55 Ma, took ~5,000 years to drop pH ~0.4; we are doing similar in ~200 years). Tropical surface waters have absorbed ~30% of anthropogenic CO2 — providing a critical sink that has reduced atmospheric CO2 but at chemical cost to marine ecosystems. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.6) require students to interpret log-scale pH change.

Invasive species are non-native organisms that establish, spread rapidly, and cause economic or ecological harm. They are the second leading cause of biodiversity loss globally (after habitat loss) per IUCN. ~50,000 non-native species are established in the US (Pimentel 2005 estimate; ~$120 billion/yr in damages and control). Reasons for invasion success: lack of co-evolved predators/pathogens/competitors in new range, high reproductive rates, broad tolerance. Pathways: ballast water (zebra mussels), pet/horticulture trade (Burmese python in Everglades, kudzu vine), hitchhikers on cargo (emerald ash borer in wood pallets). The 1900 Lacey Act and 1990 Nonindigenous Aquatic Nuisance Prevention and Control Act regulate trade. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.7).

Zebra mussels (Dreissena polymorpha) arrived in the Great Lakes in 1988 via ballast water dumped from European cargo ships. Females produce up to 1 million eggs/year and rapidly carpeted underwater surfaces, clogging water intake pipes, smothering native unionid mussels, and changing nutrient dynamics (filter-feeding clears water but concentrates pollutants).
