US economic damage estimated at >$5 billion total. Quagga mussels (D. bugensis) arrived later and now dominate deeper waters. The Nonindigenous Aquatic Nuisance Prevention and Control Act 1990 mandated USCG ballast-water regulations. NOBOB (no ballast on board) and ballast-water exchange/treatment now required. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.7) integrate invasion pathways with economic damages.

Asian carp (silver carp Hypophthalmichthys molitrix, bighead carp H. nobilis) escaped from southern US fish-farm ponds in 1980s during flooding, spread up the Mississippi River basin, and threaten the Great Lakes via the Chicago Sanitary and Ship Canal. Silver carp jump up to 3 m when startled by boat motors — injuries reported. Outcompete native fish for plankton. The US Army Corps of Engineers operates electric barriers in the Chicago canal; the 2014 GLMRIS report studied permanent hydrologic separation between Mississippi and Great Lakes basins. A new common name 'copi' (2022 IL Department of Natural Resources initiative) markets it as food. The 1900 Lacey Act and 1990 Nonindigenous Species Act provide federal authority. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.7).

Kudzu (Pueraria montana var. lobata) is a Japanese vine introduced to the US at the 1876 Philadelphia Centennial Exposition; promoted by the Soil Conservation Service (1930s-50s, paid farmers $8/acre) for erosion control in the South. Grows up to 30 cm/day (1 ft/day) — smothering trees, buildings, even abandoned cars. Now covers ~3 million ha across SE US (though recent research suggests this is overstated). Earned the nickname 'the vine that ate the South.' Reclassified as a noxious weed in 1972. Mechanical removal, herbicides (Tordon), and goat grazing are control methods. Kudzu illustrates how government-promoted introductions can become invasive disasters when species ecology is not understood. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.7) require students to predict outcomes when natural enemies are absent.

Emerald ash borer (Agrilus planipennis) is a metallic-green Asian beetle first detected in Detroit, Michigan in 2002 — likely arrived 1990s via untreated wood-pallet shipping from China. Larvae tunnel under ash bark, severing nutrient/water flow and killing trees within 2-4 years. Has killed >100 million ash trees across 36 US states; near-complete mortality of white ash (Fraxinus americana) and green ash (F. pennsylvanica) is likely. The functional loss of ash from eastern forests parallels chestnut blight (1904) and Dutch elm disease (1928). Treatments: emamectin benzoate trunk injections for high-value trees ($100-300/tree, must repeat every 2-3 years). USDA APHIS quarantines have failed to stop spread. The 2008 Farm Bill banned imported untreated wood packaging materials. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.7).

Extinction is the permanent loss of a species — the death of the last individual. Background (natural) extinction rate is ~1 species per million species per year (Pimm et al. 1995). Current human-driven rate is 100-1,000x background per IPBES 2019 — Earth is in the 'Sixth Mass Extinction' (Anthropocene extinction). Famous examples: passenger pigeon (Ectopistes migratorius, last bird Martha died Sept 1 1914 at Cincinnati Zoo — billions to zero in 50 years from hunting and habitat loss), dodo (Mauritius, ~1681), thylacine (Tasmanian tiger, last in captivity 1936), Carolina parakeet (1918), great auk (1844), Yangtze River dolphin/baiji (declared functionally extinct 2007). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.7) integrate rates, causes, and ESA protection.

questions.prompt: Loss of all members of species.
questions.explanation: Mass extinctions are global events where >75% of species disappear in geologically short time (<2 million years). Five major mass extinctions in Earth history: End-Ordovician (445 Ma, ~85% species, glaciation); Late Devonian (375 Ma, ~75%); End-Permian (252 Ma, 'Great Dying,' ~96% marine species, Siberian Traps volcanism); End-Triassic (201 Ma); End-Cretaceous (66 Ma, ~75% including non-avian dinosaurs, Chicxulub asteroid impact in Yucatan + Deccan Traps volcanism). The Sixth Extinction (current, human-driven) is unfolding 100-1,000x faster than background. Elizabeth Kolbert's 'The Sixth Extinction' (2014 Pulitzer Prize) popularized the concept. Edward O. Wilson and others document accelerating biodiversity loss. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.7) integrate.

questions.prompt: Current mass extinction caused by humans.
questions.explanation: The Holocene (or Anthropocene) extinction is the ongoing sixth mass extinction, driven by human activities — habitat loss, climate change, overharvest, pollution, invasive species (the 'HIPPO' drivers, E.O. Wilson). Current extinction rate is 100-1,000x background (IPBES Global Assessment 2019; Ceballos et al. 2015 Science Advances). Approximately 1 million species face extinction in coming decades (IPBES). Megafauna extinctions began ~50,000 years ago with human dispersal (Australian megafauna ~46,000 ya, American megafauna ~13,000 ya — overhunting + climate). Wilson estimated ~27,000 species/yr lost in 1990s tropical deforestation. The proposed 'Anthropocene' epoch (Paul Crutzen 2000) reflects human geologic-scale impact. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.7) integrate causes and policy.

questions.prompt: Species at risk but not extinct.
questions.explanation: Endangered species face imminent risk of extinction across all or significant portion of their range. The IUCN Red List (since 1964) classifies into nine categories: Extinct, Extinct in Wild, Critically Endangered, Endangered, Vulnerable, Near Threatened, Least Concern, Data Deficient, Not Evaluated. ~28% of assessed species (>44,000 species as of 2023) are threatened. US Endangered Species Act (1973) lists ~1,700 US species as Endangered or Threatened. Success stories under ESA: bald eagle (delisted 2007 after DDT ban 1972 and protection), peregrine falcon (delisted 1999), gray wolf (recovering since 1995 Yellowstone reintroduction), American alligator (1987), brown pelican (2009). Manatees, California condors, red wolves still struggling. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.7).

The Endangered Species Act (ESA), signed by President Nixon December 28, 1973, is the strongest federal law protecting biodiversity. Section 4 lists species as Endangered or Threatened; Section 7 requires federal agencies to consult on actions affecting listed species; Section 9 prohibits 'take' (killing, harming, harassing) of listed species; Section 10 allows Habitat Conservation Plans (HCPs) for incidental take. ~1,700 US species currently listed. Famous cases: Tennessee Valley Authority v. Hill (1978) halted Tellico Dam to protect the snail darter; Northern Spotted Owl protected old-growth Pacific NW forests (1990); gray wolf reintroduction to Yellowstone (1995) restored trophic cascades. Recovery success rate ~99% — only ~1% of listed species have gone extinct since 1973. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.7).

Protected areas are geographic spaces dedicated to long-term conservation of biodiversity, ecosystem services, and cultural values. IUCN classifies six categories from strict nature reserves (Ia) to sustainable-use areas (VI). Globally, ~17% of terrestrial and ~8% of marine areas are protected (Convention on Biological Diversity Aichi Target 11 by 2020; 30x30 target of 30% by 2030, adopted at COP15 in Montreal 2022). The US National Wildlife Refuge System (since 1903 — Pelican Island FL was first, designated by Theodore Roosevelt) covers >850 refuges. National Parks (62 in US, started with Yellowstone 1872), National Forests, BLM wilderness all contribute. Marine Protected Areas (MPAs) cover ~8% of oceans. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.8) integrate area metrics with biodiversity outcomes.

Yellowstone National Park, established March 1, 1872 by President Ulysses S. Grant under the Yellowstone Act, was the world's first national park — 2.2 million acres of geysers (Old Faithful), hot springs, and wildlife straddling Wyoming, Montana, and Idaho. Yellowstone catalyzed the global national-park movement now spanning ~6,000 parks. The 1995-96 reintroduction of gray wolves (extirpated by 1926) restored the ecosystem's apex predator — wolves reduced elk grazing pressure on aspen and willow, allowing riparian regeneration that altered stream morphology (the textbook trophic cascade). Yellowstone hosts the largest US elk herds, ~5,500 bison (the only continuous wild bison population since prehistory), grizzly bears (delisted 2017, relisted by court). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.8).

Wildlife corridors are strips of habitat connecting isolated reserves, allowing animals to move, find mates, and recolonize after local extinctions. Counters habitat fragmentation — the leading conservation challenge per IUCN. Famous corridors: Yellowstone to Yukon Conservation Initiative (Y2Y, 2,000 mile corridor spanning Rocky Mountains since 1993), Path of the Pronghorn (200 mile annual migration corridor in WY/MT), Florida Panther wildlife crossings under Alligator Alley (I-75) reduced roadkill ~90%. Banff National Park wildlife crossings (over and under Trans-Canada Highway, 38 since 1996) reduced wildlife-vehicle collisions 80% and used by 11+ large-mammal species. Mountain Lion P-22 in Los Angeles became poster animal for the Wallis Annenberg Wildlife Crossing on US 101 (under construction). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.8).

Captive breeding (ex situ conservation) breeds endangered species in zoos, aquariums, and reserves with the goal of reintroduction to the wild. The Association of Zoos and Aquariums (AZA) Species Survival Plans coordinate breeding to maintain genetic diversity. Major successes: California condor (Gymnogyps californianus — reduced to 22 wild birds 1982, captive bred at San Diego Zoo and Los Angeles Zoo, now 500+ with reintroductions in CA, AZ, UT, Baja CA); black-footed ferret (down to 18 wild individuals 1985, now 500+); Arabian oryx (extinct in wild 1972, reintroduced 1982); Przewalski's horse (extinct in wild 1969, reintroduced to Mongolia 1992). Limitations: high cost, behavior loss, genetic bottleneck, disease introduction on release. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.8) integrate with reintroduction.

Reintroduction releases captive-bred or translocated animals into their historical range to restore extirpated populations. IUCN guidelines (1998, updated 2013) require ecological need, habitat sufficiency, threat removal, and population viability before reintroduction. Famous successes: gray wolves to Yellowstone (1995-96, restored trophic cascade); California condors to Grand Canyon/Big Sur/Baja (1992+, ~500 today); whooping cranes (Wisconsin to Florida ultralight-led migration, since 2001); American beaver across Europe (~1.5 million today after extirpation by 1800); Eurasian lynx to Switzerland (1971), Czechia, Germany. Failures: red wolf in NC (declining since 2010s due to coyote hybridization). The 1973 Endangered Species Act funds reintroduction through Section 6 grants. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.8) integrate captive breeding to wild restoration.

Biodiversity encompasses three nested scales: genetic diversity within species, species diversity within communities, and ecosystem diversity across landscapes. E.O. Wilson (the 'father of biodiversity' who popularized the term in 1988) estimated 8.7 million species exist (most undescribed). The Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD, 1992 Rio Earth Summit) is the global treaty; the 2022 Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework set the 30x30 target (30% of land/sea protected by 2030). Current extinction rate is 100-1,000x background per IPBES 2019 — Earth's Sixth Mass Extinction. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 2.1) require students to integrate the three scales, connect to ecosystem services and resilience, and propose conservation strategies.

Approximately 90% of energy is lost as metabolic heat (cellular respiration, Second Law of Thermodynamics), undigested waste, and incomplete consumption between trophic levels — leaving only ~10% to incorporate into the next level's biomass. Raymond Lindeman established this quantitatively in his foundational 1942 paper 'The trophic-dynamic aspect of ecology' (Ecology 23:399-417) — he died at 27 just before publication. This is why food chains rarely exceed 4-5 levels (energy runs out) and why eating lower on the food chain is more energetically efficient (~10x more people fed per acre on plants vs meat). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 1.7) demand iterative application: 100,000 kcal producer base supports 10,000 herbivore, 1,000 small carnivore, 100 apex predator.

Photosynthesis converts solar energy to chemical energy: 6 CO2 + 6 H2O + light yields C6H12O6 + 6 O2. Joseph Priestley discovered oxygen release in 1771; Jan Ingenhousz showed light requirement in 1779. Captures ~1-3% of incoming solar radiation. Drives gross primary productivity (GPP); subtracting plant respiration gives net primary productivity (NPP). Highest NPP in tropical rainforests (~2,200 g C/m^2/yr); lowest in deserts and tundra (<200). Marine phytoplankton (especially diatoms and cyanobacteria) contribute ~50% of global photosynthesis. The Great Oxidation Event ~2.4 Ga began with cyanobacterial photosynthesis releasing O2. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 1.3, 1.4) require students to write the equation, distinguish GPP from NPP, and integrate with the carbon cycle.

Carrying capacity (K) is the maximum population size an environment can sustainably support, determined by limiting resources (food, water, space, nesting sites). In logistic growth dN/dt = rN(1 - N/K), K is the asymptote. Garrett Hardin's 'Tragedy of the Commons' (Science 1968) showed unregulated common-resource access drives overshoot of K and crash. St. Matthew Island reindeer: 29 introduced 1944, grew to 6,000 by 1963, crashed to 42 by 1966 (~99% mortality, David Klein 1968). Atlantic cod off Newfoundland: 99% population loss by 1992 from overfishing. Global ecological footprint (Wackernagel and Rees) is ~1.7 Earths — humanity is in overshoot. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 3.4, 3.7) integrate K with logistic growth, MSY, and sustainability.

Extinction is the permanent loss of all members of a species. Background rate ~1 species/million/yr; current human-driven rate is 100-1,000x faster (IPBES 2019 Global Assessment) — the Sixth Mass Extinction or Anthropocene extinction. Famous extinctions: passenger pigeon (~5 billion in 1850 to extinct by 1914, Martha died at Cincinnati Zoo Sept 1 1914 — from market hunting); dodo (Mauritius, 1681); great auk (1844); thylacine (1936). E.O. Wilson estimated 27,000 species/yr lost in 1990s tropical deforestation. Drivers (HIPPO): Habitat loss, Invasive species, Pollution, Population (human), Overexploitation. The Endangered Species Act (1973) prevents listed-species extinction with ~99% success rate. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.7) integrate causes.

Natural gas (CH4) emits ~50% less CO2 per unit energy than coal and no SO2 or mercury — combustion: CH4 + 2 O2 yields CO2 + 2 H2O. Generates ~23% of global electricity (rising as gas displaces coal). US shale-gas boom since 2008 via hydraulic fracturing (Marcellus, Bakken, Permian) made US the world's #1 producer by 2011 and drove US power-sector CO2 down ~30% from 2005-2020. Concerns: fugitive methane emissions (CH4 has ~80x CO2 GWP over 20 years; 1-3% leakage offsets climate benefits), groundwater concerns, induced seismicity (Oklahoma wastewater-injection earthquakes rose from 50/yr to 900+ in 2015). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 6.7) demand integrated trade-off analysis vs renewables and other fossil fuels.

Nuclear fission splits heavy atomic nuclei (U-235 or Pu-239) into lighter fragments, releasing 200 MeV per event — roughly 50 million times more energy per atom than chemical combustion. Discovered by Otto Hahn, Lise Meitner, and Fritz Strassmann (1938). Controlled in reactors with moderators (water, graphite) and control rods (cadmium, boron). Provides ~10% of global electricity (~440 reactors). Zero CO2 emissions during operation. Major accidents: Three Mile Island 1979 (no deaths, contained partial meltdown), Chernobyl 1986 (reactor explosion, ~4,000-93,000 long-term deaths estimated), Fukushima 2011 (Tohoku earthquake/tsunami caused triple meltdown). Spent fuel radioactive for 240,000+ years requires geologic disposal (Finland's Onkalo operational 2025). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 6.10).

Solar photovoltaic (PV) converts sunlight directly to electricity via the photoelectric effect (Einstein Nobel 1921). Crystalline silicon dominates (~95% market); efficiency rose from ~6% (Bell Labs 1954) to 22-24% commercial today, with multi-junction lab cells exceeding 47%. Costs collapsed >99% from $76/W in 1977 to ~$0.20/W today — the fastest-falling energy technology in history (Swanson's Law: ~20% cost reduction per doubling of capacity). Global installed capacity reached ~1.6 TW in 2024 (~6% of electricity). Benefits: zero operational emissions, modular scale, no water use. Challenges: intermittency requires storage or backup, end-of-life recycling, manufacturing chemicals (cadmium). Inflation Reduction Act 2022 invests $369B in clean energy. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 6.11).

The Green Revolution (1940s-1970s) tripled global agricultural yields through Norman Borlaug's semi-dwarf wheat varieties (developed at Mexico's CIMMYT — short stalks resist lodging under heavy grain), high-yield rice (IRRI's IR8 'miracle rice' 1966), synthetic fertilizers (Haber-Bosch N), pesticides, and irrigation. Borlaug won the Nobel Peace Prize 1970, credited with saving an estimated 1 billion lives. Indian wheat production tripled 1965-1980; India shifted from famine-prone importer to exporter. M.S. Swaminathan led India's adoption. Costs: aquifer depletion (Punjab water table falling 1 m/yr), soil salinization, biodiversity loss (genetic uniformity vulnerable to disease — Irish potato famine parallel), fertilizer runoff causing dead zones, displacement of subsistence farmers. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 5.3).

Topsoil (A horizon) is the upper mineral soil layer mixing organic matter with minerals — dark, nutrient-rich, biologically active with bacteria, fungi, earthworms, arthropods. Forms slowly (~1 cm per 500-1000 years from rock weathering and organic accumulation) — non-renewable on human timescales. The Dust Bowl (1930s, Hugh Hammond Bennett's testimony) eroded ~75 million acres of US Great Plains topsoil after deep plowing of native grassland — prompting the 1935 Soil Conservation Act and creation of the Soil Conservation Service. Globally, ~24 billion tons of topsoil lost annually (FAO 2020). Conservation practices: cover crops, no-till farming, contour plowing, terracing, crop rotation, riparian buffers. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 4.4, 5.2) integrate soil with sustainable agriculture and food security.

The Montreal Protocol on Substances that Deplete the Ozone Layer (signed September 16, 1987, entered force January 1, 1989) phases out CFCs, halons, methyl bromide, HCFCs. Universally ratified by 198 parties — the only environmental treaty with this status. Triggered by Joe Farman et al.'s 1985 discovery of the Antarctic ozone hole and the 1995 Nobel Prize work of Mario Molina, F. Sherwood Rowland, and Paul Crutzen establishing CFC-O3 destruction mechanism (each Cl atom can destroy ~100,000 O3 molecules). Atmospheric CFC concentrations peaked ~1994 and are now declining; ozone layer projected to fully recover by 2070 (WMO 2022). Co-benefit: avoided ~135 GtCO2-equivalent emissions (CFCs are potent GHGs) — larger than Kyoto Protocol reductions. The 2016 Kigali Amendment phases out HFCs. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 7.7).

The Clean Air Act (CAA), signed by President Nixon on December 31, 1970, gave the newly created EPA (also 1970) authority to set National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS) for six Criteria Pollutants: particulate matter (PM2.5, PM10), ozone, CO, SO2, NOx, and lead. Lead phaseout (1973-1995) saw US blood-lead levels drop ~93%. The 1977 amendments added PSD and NSR. The 1990 amendments (George H.W. Bush) established Title IV SO2 cap-and-trade (acid rain — reduced SO2 ~92% by 2020), Title VI ozone-depleting substances, and Title III hazardous air pollutants. EPA cost-benefit analysis (1999): CAA saved 230,000 lives/yr by 2020 with ~30:1 benefit-cost ratio ($1.3 trillion benefits vs $50 billion costs). Massachusetts v. EPA (2007 Supreme Court) included CO2 under CAA. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 7.5).

questions.prompt: Pollutant from coal causing acid rain.
questions.explanation: Sulfur dioxide (SO2) is the primary cause of acid rain — formed by combusting sulfur-containing fossil fuels (especially high-sulfur Appalachian coal, 0.5-5% S) and metal smelting. SO2 + O2 + H2O yields H2SO4 (sulfuric acid). The 1990 Clean Air Act Amendments (George H.W. Bush) Title IV established SO2 cap-and-trade Acid Rain Program — EPA capped national power-plant SO2, distributed tradable allowances, let market find lowest-cost reductions. US SO2 emissions dropped ~92% from 1990 (~16 million tons) to 2020 (~1.3 million tons). Benefit-cost ratio 25-40:1 ($122 billion/yr benefits vs $3 billion/yr costs). The 1952 London 'Great Smog' (coal-SO2 yellow fog) killed 4,000-12,000 and prompted UK's 1956 Clean Air Act. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 7.5).

Biomagnification (food-chain magnification) is the increasing concentration of persistent, fat-soluble toxins at each ascending trophic level — typically ~10x per level. Classic DDT cascade documented by Woodwell et al. (1967): Long Island estuary water 0.000003 ppm, zooplankton 0.04 ppm, small fish 0.5 ppm, large fish 2 ppm, fish-eating birds 25 ppm — a 10-million-fold concentration. Bald eagles, brown pelicans, ospreys, and peregrine falcons suffered eggshell-thinning from DDT-DDE, approaching extinction by 1970. Rachel Carson's 'Silent Spring' (1962) catalyzed public response; William Ruckelshaus at EPA banned DDT in 1972; raptor populations recovered. Other biomagnifying toxins: PCBs, methylmercury (Minamata 1956), dioxins, PFAS. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 8.7) require quantitative trophic-level concentration calculations.

Eutrophication is the over-enrichment of water with N/P nutrients causing explosive algal/cyanobacterial growth. Natural eutrophication takes millennia; cultural eutrophication from agriculture, sewage, detergents (banned in US laundry 1994) accelerates it to years. Process: nutrient loading yields algal bloom; algae die; bacterial decomposition consumes dissolved oxygen (BOD spike) creating hypoxic dead zone (DO <2 mg/L) suffocating fish. The Gulf of Mexico dead zone from Mississippi River agricultural runoff averages ~6,000 mi^2 annually (peaked 22,720 mi^2 in 2017). Lake Erie 2014 cyanobacteria bloom (microcystin toxin) shut down Toledo's drinking water for 500,000 residents. Chesapeake Bay TMDL since 2010 targets N/P reduction. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 8.4, 8.5) require students to integrate.

The Paris Agreement was adopted at COP21 (the 21st Conference of Parties to the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change) on December 12, 2015, in Paris, entering force November 4, 2016. 196 parties pledged Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) to limit global warming 'well below 2 degC' above pre-industrial, with aspirational 1.5 degC target. Bottom-up structure (countries set own targets with 5-year ratchet) replaced top-down Kyoto Protocol (1997) approach that excluded developing countries. The US withdrew under Trump (2017-2021), rejoined under Biden (Feb 19, 2021). Current NDCs collectively put world on ~2.7 degC track — short of the goal. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.10) require students to evaluate Paris vs Kyoto effectiveness and propose strengthening mechanisms.

CO2 is the dominant human-caused greenhouse gas — responsible for ~76% of anthropogenic GHG warming (IPCC AR6 2021). Atmospheric concentration has risen from pre-industrial ~280 ppm (Antarctic ice cores: EPICA Dome C 800,000-year record, Luthi et al. 2008 Nature) to ~420 ppm by 2024 — 50% increase, highest in 3+ million years. Charles David Keeling began continuous measurements at Mauna Loa, Hawaii in 1958 (the Keeling Curve). Atmospheric lifetime ~300-1,000 years (some persists 100,000+ years), so CO2 accumulates. Sources: fossil-fuel combustion (~75% of global emissions), deforestation (~10%), cement production (~5%). Svante Arrhenius (1896) first calculated greenhouse warming. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.3, 9.4) integrate physics, monitoring, and mitigation.

Extinction is the permanent loss of a species — death of the last individual. Background rate ~1 species per million per year (Pimm et al. 1995); current human-driven rate is 100-1,000x background (IPBES 2019 Global Assessment, Ceballos et al. 2015 Science Advances) — the Sixth Mass Extinction or Anthropocene extinction. Approximately 1 million species face extinction in coming decades (IPBES). Drivers (HIPPO, E.O. Wilson): Habitat loss, Invasive species, Pollution, Population (human), Overexploitation. Famous extinctions: passenger pigeon (5 billion to extinct by 1914); dodo (Mauritius 1681); thylacine (1936); great auk (1844); baiji Yangtze dolphin (functionally extinct 2007). Elizabeth Kolbert's 'The Sixth Extinction' (2014 Pulitzer Prize). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.7).

The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) was established 1988 by UNEP and the World Meteorological Organization (WMO). ~200 scientists synthesize peer-reviewed literature across three Working Groups (physical science, impacts/adaptation, mitigation). Six Assessment Reports (1990, 1995, 2001, 2007, 2014, 2021-23). AR6 conclusions: human-caused warming has reached ~1.1 degC above pre-industrial; warming will reach 1.5 degC by ~2030-2035; limiting to 1.5 degC requires global emissions peak by 2025 and halve by 2030; net zero by 2050. Shared 2007 Nobel Peace Prize with Al Gore. IPCC findings directly shaped Kyoto Protocol (1997) and Paris Agreement (2015). AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.4, 9.10) integrate IPCC science with international climate policy.

The Endangered Species Act (ESA), signed by President Nixon December 28, 1973, is the strongest federal law protecting biodiversity. Section 4 lists species as Endangered or Threatened; Section 7 requires federal agencies to consult on actions affecting listed species; Section 9 prohibits 'take' (killing, harming, harassing); Section 10 allows Habitat Conservation Plans (HCPs) for incidental take. ~1,700 US species currently listed. Famous cases: Tennessee Valley Authority v. Hill (1978, snail darter halted Tellico Dam); Northern Spotted Owl (1990, protected old-growth Pacific NW forests); bald eagle (delisted 2007 after DDT ban 1972); gray wolf reintroduction to Yellowstone (1995); peregrine falcon (delisted 1999). Recovery success rate ~99% — only ~1% of listed species have gone extinct since 1973. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.7).

Ecosystem services are the free, often invisible benefits humans receive from functioning ecosystems. The 2005 Millennium Ecosystem Assessment categorized them into four classes: provisioning (food, water, fiber), regulating (climate, flood, disease control), supporting (nutrient cycling, soil formation, photosynthesis), cultural (recreation, aesthetic, spiritual). Robert Costanza (1997 Nature paper) estimated global ecosystem-service value at $33 trillion/year — roughly double global GDP at the time; updated 2014 estimate is ~$125 trillion/yr. Bee pollination alone valued at $235-577 billion globally (IPBES 2016). Coastal wetlands averted $625 million in Hurricane Sandy 2012 damages. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 1.5) require students to categorize services and argue economic justifications for conservation.

questions.prompt: Cycle moving N from atmosphere to organisms.
questions.explanation: The nitrogen cycle moves N between atmosphere (78% N2 inert), biosphere (organic compounds), and lithosphere. Five key processes: (1) N-fixation by Rhizobium bacteria in legume root nodules (and lightning ~5%, and the industrial Haber-Bosch process — Fritz Haber Nobel 1918 — which now fixes more N annually than all natural processes combined); (2) nitrification by Nitrosomonas (NH4+ to NO2-) and Nitrobacter (NO2- to NO3-); (3) assimilation by plants (NO3- and NH4+ uptake); (4) ammonification by decomposers; (5) denitrification by anaerobic bacteria (NO3- to N2). Synthetic fertilizers cause eutrophication and the Gulf of Mexico dead zone. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 1.10) require students to name all five processes and identify Rhizobium.

Logistic growth: dN/dt = rN(1 - N/K), producing an S-shaped sigmoidal curve. Pierre-Francois Verhulst derived it 1838; Raymond Pearl rediscovered and applied 1920 with Drosophila experiments. Population grows exponentially when N << K, decelerates as N approaches K, stabilizes at N = K. Maximum sustainable yield (MSY) occurs at N = K/2 — the harvest-management target under the Magnuson-Stevens Fishery Conservation and Management Act (1976). The Atlantic cod collapse off Newfoundland (1992 — 99% population loss prompting Canadian moratorium that put 30,000 fishers out of work, still not recovered) shows what happens when harvest exceeds MSY long-term. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 3.4, 5.10) require students to calculate growth at various N/K ratios and identify MSY.

Invasive species are non-native organisms that establish, spread rapidly, and cause economic or ecological harm. Second leading cause of biodiversity loss globally (after habitat loss) per IUCN. ~50,000 non-native species are established in the US, causing ~$120 billion/yr damages (Pimentel 2005). Success factors: lack of co-evolved predators/pathogens/competitors, high reproductive rates, broad tolerance. Pathways: ballast water (zebra mussels 1988 Great Lakes), pet/horticulture trade (Burmese python in Everglades, kudzu vine since 1876 Centennial Exposition), hitchhikers on cargo (emerald ash borer in wood pallets, 2002 Detroit). The 1900 Lacey Act, 1990 Nonindigenous Aquatic Nuisance Prevention and Control Act, and 1992 NISA regulate trade. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 9.7).

Urbanization is the demographic shift from rural to urban living. In 1800, only 3% of humans lived in cities; in 1900, 14%; in 2008 humanity crossed 50% urban (UN-Habitat); by 2050, 68% projected. Megacities (>10 million population): Tokyo (37M), Delhi (32M), Shanghai (28M), Sao Paulo (22M), Mexico City (22M), Cairo (21M). Urbanization brings benefits — lower per-capita CO2 emissions, better education/healthcare access, lower fertility — and costs: slum formation (~1 billion in informal settlements per UN), urban heat island effect (cities 2-5 degC warmer than rural surroundings, intensifying heat-wave mortality), food/water/waste pressures, loss of agricultural land. Green infrastructure (urban wetlands, rain gardens, permeable pavement) mitigates impacts. AP Environmental Science FRQs (CED Unit 5.16).
